title: “HUGEN 2072 Project 2” output: html_document: code_folding: show toc: true toc_float: true —s

Instructions

Below is a tutorial consisting of a mostly-completed QC pipeline and some prompts.

Project 1 has three components:

  • Scripting/coding: read through this file and complete the pipeline by contributing your code where indicated.

  • Narrative: knit the completed .Rmd to a code-integrated HTML report of your QC process and decisions.

    • Your report must answer all the questions and perform all the tasks indicated in the instructions/prompts given for each step.

    • You must submit a successfully-knitted HTML file and the .Rmd in order to earn a passing grade for this project.

    • Set up your report to flow like an actual document you might show your supervisor to summarize the QC project, emphasizing what was done at each step rather than the code used to do it (e.g., how many samples/SNPs were filtered out at a given step?)

    • Submit your .Rmd and HTML to Canvas

  • Presentation: you will make a 10 min (maximum) recording of yourself with Panopto in which you summarize this QC pipeline. Instructions will given on Canvas.

In each Part of this project you will implement one of the main steps of cleaning genotyping array data in a small dataset (note that we left a few steps out). You’ll be shown how to use functions from the {GWASTools} package, and you’ll supply a little of your own code to complete the pipeline that’s been provided. At each step, you may be asked to create a filter for the data, to address errors/discrepancies, etc. Then for each step you’ll make a short report (a few sentences and figures) explaining what you did.

Note that “sample” and “scan” are used interchangeably throughout this assignment, but that these are NOT synonymous with subject/participant/individual/etc. In this assignment, “SNP” and “probe” are used interchangeably, but in other contexts you should always remember the distinction.

Since the pipeline below is incomplete, some of the R code chunks are set to eval=FALSE so that knitting to HTML will succeed. You will need to change them to eval=TRUE as you progress so that your knitted document shows the results of running each chunk.

It is expected that you will copy this file somewhere to your directory on the CRC cluster so that you can use the RStudio Server, where all the packages you need have been installed already.

Acknowlegement

This work was done is parallel with Kash, Caylin, Vanessa, and Cecileigh. However, I take ultimate responsibility for the content of this assignment.

Part 0 - Setup and Introduction to Annotation Data Frames

First, load the packages we’ll be using.

# Load the required packages for the project (Install them if necessary)

library("GWASTools", quietly = TRUE)
library("GWASdata", quietly = TRUE)
library("SNPRelate", quietly = TRUE)

# Close any open GDS files before proceeding (think of this as starting off with
# a 'blank slate')

showfile.gds(closeall = T, verbose = T)

The array data (genotypes, intensities, and BAF/LRR information) for this project are contained in several GDS files; accompanying SNP and scan annotation are also provided as an .RData file.

The probe/SNP annotation is stored in an object called a scan annotation data frame, and the sample-level annotation is stored in a scan annotation data frame. Each of these objects is similar to an ordinary data frame. More precisely, a data frame is embedded within each. There is also accompanying metadata. To access the data and metadata, the {GWASTools} package provides several functions. Three basic functions you should familiarize yourself with are getAnnotation()/pData() (extracts a standard data frame containing the annotation), getMetadata()/varMetadata() (extracts a data frame of variable descriptions), and getVariableNames()/varLabels() (extracts a vector of just the variable names). You can also view a variable in one of these objects by using $, as you would for an ordinary data frame. You can also add new variables, but you need to be careful to update the metadata as well. Since we will be adding new variables in the process of cleaning our data, the following chunk of code shows you how to do this. Then you will practice looking at the annotation, and your task is to answer some questions about this data set.

First, load the annotation data (annotation_data.RData) that was provided with this project. You need to supply the path to this file.

# Load the probe (SNP) and sample annotation data frames, which have been saved
# in an .RData file

path_to_annotation_data <-
  "/ix1/hugen2072-2026s/p1/annotation_data.RData"
load(path_to_annotation_data)

# List the loaded objects

ls()
## [1] "path_to_annotation_data" "scanAnnot"              
## [3] "snpAnnot"

Here’s a toy example showing you how to work with annotation data frames. Read this carefully, since you’ll need to do this throughout the assignment.

### How to add a new variable to an annotation data frame ###

#   Make a new copy of the SNP annotation (just for this example)

snp_annodf_temp <- snpAnnot

#   Notice that printing the object isn't that helpful

snp_annodf_temp
## An object of class 'SnpAnnotationDataFrame'
##   snps: 1 2 ... 3300 (3300 total)
##   varLabels: snpID chromosome ... rBB (14 total)
##   varMetadata: labelDescription
#   So extract the annotation itself (this is the 'normal' data frame embedded
#   in the SNP annotation data frame object). Now you can see the SNP-level
#   annotation

snp_data_temp <- getAnnotation(snp_annodf_temp)
head(snp_data_temp)
##    snpID chromosome position      rsID alleleA alleleB  BeadSetID IntensityOnly
## 1 999447         21 13733610 rs3132407       A       G 1185447327             1
## 2 999465         21 13852569 rs2775671       T       C 1169708488             0
## 3 999493         21 14038583 rs2775018       T       C 1192445330             0
## 4 999512         21 14136579 rs3115511       T       C 1149617207             0
## 5 999561         21 14396024 rs2822404       T       C 1149961944             0
## 6 999567         21 14404476 rs1556276       A       G 1149617207             0
##           tAA       tAB       tBB       rAA       rAB       rBB
## 1 0.013743570 0.3290431 0.9184624 1.5622030 1.5927530 1.6142590
## 2 0.063259460 0.5440393 0.9796721 0.4431986 0.4431986 0.4431986
## 3 0.001315146 0.2623954 0.5362323 2.6349810 2.3149340 2.0012760
## 4 0.011004820 0.5692499 0.9846884 0.8781826 0.9453412 0.8209958
## 5 0.040206810 0.5691788 0.9902423 1.0941700 1.1270790 0.9898759
## 6 0.030895730 0.6842008 0.9837771 0.5954081 0.7681253 0.7900150
#   Add a new column to the annotation (an integer dummy variable)

snp_data_temp$dummy <- 1:nrow(snp_data_temp)

#   Now update the scan annotation object itself by replacing the what was in
#   the annotation "slot"

pData(snp_annodf_temp) <- snp_data_temp

#   You can see that the new column has been added

head(pData(snp_annodf_temp))
##    snpID chromosome position      rsID alleleA alleleB  BeadSetID IntensityOnly
## 1 999447         21 13733610 rs3132407       A       G 1185447327             1
## 2 999465         21 13852569 rs2775671       T       C 1169708488             0
## 3 999493         21 14038583 rs2775018       T       C 1192445330             0
## 4 999512         21 14136579 rs3115511       T       C 1149617207             0
## 5 999561         21 14396024 rs2822404       T       C 1149961944             0
## 6 999567         21 14404476 rs1556276       A       G 1149617207             0
##           tAA       tAB       tBB       rAA       rAB       rBB dummy
## 1 0.013743570 0.3290431 0.9184624 1.5622030 1.5927530 1.6142590     1
## 2 0.063259460 0.5440393 0.9796721 0.4431986 0.4431986 0.4431986     2
## 3 0.001315146 0.2623954 0.5362323 2.6349810 2.3149340 2.0012760     3
## 4 0.011004820 0.5692499 0.9846884 0.8781826 0.9453412 0.8209958     4
## 5 0.040206810 0.5691788 0.9902423 1.0941700 1.1270790 0.9898759     5
## 6 0.030895730 0.6842008 0.9837771 0.5954081 0.7681253 0.7900150     6
#   Extract the metadata so that it can be updated

meta_temp <- getMetadata(snp_annodf_temp)
meta_temp
##                                                                                                        labelDescription
## snpID                                                                                        unique integer ID for SNPs
## chromosome    integer code for chromosome: 1:22=autosomes, 23=X, 24=pseudoautosomal, 25=Y, 26=Mitochondrial, 27=Unknown
## position                                                                    base pair position on chromosome (build 36)
## rsID                                                                                                      RS identifier
## alleleA                                                                                                         alelleA
## alleleB                                                                                                         alleleB
## BeadSetID                                                                                      BeadSet ID from Illumina
## IntensityOnly                                                              1=no genotypes were attempted for this assay
## tAA                                                                                           mean theta for AA cluster
## tAB                                                                                           mean theta for AB cluster
## tBB                                                                                           mean theta for BB cluster
## rAA                                                                                               mean R for AA cluster
## rAB                                                                                               mean R for AB cluster
## rBB                                                                                               mean R for BB cluster
## dummy                                                                                                              <NA>
#   Add a new row titled "dummy" with an appropriate labelDescription (note that
#   the metadata is a data frame with named rows but one column)

meta_temp["dummy", "labelDescription"] <- "This is a dummy variable"

#   Finally, replace the old metadata with the new metadata

varMetadata(snp_annodf_temp) <- meta_temp

#   Look at the data and metadata to make sure there the dummy variable has been
#   added

head(getAnnotation(snp_annodf_temp))
##    snpID chromosome position      rsID alleleA alleleB  BeadSetID IntensityOnly
## 1 999447         21 13733610 rs3132407       A       G 1185447327             1
## 2 999465         21 13852569 rs2775671       T       C 1169708488             0
## 3 999493         21 14038583 rs2775018       T       C 1192445330             0
## 4 999512         21 14136579 rs3115511       T       C 1149617207             0
## 5 999561         21 14396024 rs2822404       T       C 1149961944             0
## 6 999567         21 14404476 rs1556276       A       G 1149617207             0
##           tAA       tAB       tBB       rAA       rAB       rBB dummy
## 1 0.013743570 0.3290431 0.9184624 1.5622030 1.5927530 1.6142590     1
## 2 0.063259460 0.5440393 0.9796721 0.4431986 0.4431986 0.4431986     2
## 3 0.001315146 0.2623954 0.5362323 2.6349810 2.3149340 2.0012760     3
## 4 0.011004820 0.5692499 0.9846884 0.8781826 0.9453412 0.8209958     4
## 5 0.040206810 0.5691788 0.9902423 1.0941700 1.1270790 0.9898759     5
## 6 0.030895730 0.6842008 0.9837771 0.5954081 0.7681253 0.7900150     6
getMetadata(snp_annodf_temp)
##                                                                                                        labelDescription
## snpID                                                                                        unique integer ID for SNPs
## chromosome    integer code for chromosome: 1:22=autosomes, 23=X, 24=pseudoautosomal, 25=Y, 26=Mitochondrial, 27=Unknown
## position                                                                    base pair position on chromosome (build 36)
## rsID                                                                                                      RS identifier
## alleleA                                                                                                         alelleA
## alleleB                                                                                                         alleleB
## BeadSetID                                                                                      BeadSet ID from Illumina
## IntensityOnly                                                              1=no genotypes were attempted for this assay
## tAA                                                                                           mean theta for AA cluster
## tAB                                                                                           mean theta for AB cluster
## tBB                                                                                           mean theta for BB cluster
## rAA                                                                                               mean R for AA cluster
## rAB                                                                                               mean R for AB cluster
## rBB                                                                                               mean R for BB cluster
## dummy                                                                                          This is a dummy variable
#   Remove the temp files

rm(snp_annodf_temp, snp_data_temp, meta_temp)

Now we use GdsGenotypeReader() and GdsIntensityReader() to open the gds files that contain the array data. Then use GenotypeData() and IntensityData() to combine them with the SNP and sample annotation, since the functions we’ll be using later require this information to be “packaged” together.

#   Get the genotypes from a gds file

genofile <-
  system.file("extdata", "illumina_geno.gds", package = "GWASdata")
gds_geno <- GdsGenotypeReader(genofile)

#   Combine the genotypes with the sample and SNP annotation

genoData <-
  GenotypeData(gds_geno, snpAnnot = snpAnnot, scanAnnot = scanAnnot)
genoData
## An object of class GenotypeData 
##  | data:
## File: /software/rhel9/manual/install/r/4.5.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_geno.gds (94.0K)
## +    [  ]
## |--+ sample.id   { Int32 77 ZIP(42.2%), 130B }
## |--+ snp.id   { Int32 3300 ZIP(38.5%), 5.0K }
## |--+ snp.chromosome   { UInt8 3300 ZIP(1.15%), 38B } *
## |--+ snp.position   { Int32 3300 ZIP(91.0%), 11.7K }
## |--+ snp.rs.id   { VStr8 3300 ZIP(38.4%), 12.5K }
## |--+ snp.allele   { VStr8 3300 ZIP(9.49%), 1.2K }
## \--+ genotype   { Bit2 3300x77, 62.0K } *
##  | SNP Annotation:
## An object of class 'SnpAnnotationDataFrame'
##   snps: 1 2 ... 3300 (3300 total)
##   varLabels: snpID chromosome ... rBB (14 total)
##   varMetadata: labelDescription
##  | Scan Annotation:
## An object of class 'ScanAnnotationDataFrame'
##   scans: 1 2 ... 77 (77 total)
##   varLabels: scanID subjectID ... file (13 total)
##   varMetadata: labelDescription
#   Get the intensity data from a gds file

qxyfile <-
  system.file("extdata", "illumina_qxy.gds", package = "GWASdata")
gds_qxy <- GdsIntensityReader(qxyfile)

#   Combine with the sample and SNP annotation

qxyData <-
  IntensityData(gds_qxy, snpAnnot = snpAnnot, scanAnnot = scanAnnot)
qxyData
## An object of class IntensityData 
##  | data:
## File: /software/rhel9/manual/install/r/4.5.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_qxy.gds (1.0M)
## +    [  ]
## |--+ sample.id   { Int32 77 ZIP(42.2%), 130B }
## |--+ snp.id   { Int32 3300 ZIP(38.5%), 5.0K }
## |--+ snp.chromosome   { UInt8 3300 ZIP(1.15%), 38B } *
## |--+ snp.position   { Int32 3300 ZIP(91.0%), 11.7K }
## |--+ snp.rs.id   { VStr8 3300 ZIP(38.4%), 12.5K }
## |--+ quality   { Float32 3300x77 ZIP(4.93%), 48.9K }
## |--+ X   { Float32 3300x77 ZIP(50.3%), 498.8K }
## \--+ Y   { Float32 3300x77 ZIP(49.4%), 490.5K }
##  | SNP Annotation:
## An object of class 'SnpAnnotationDataFrame'
##   snps: 1 2 ... 3300 (3300 total)
##   varLabels: snpID chromosome ... rBB (14 total)
##   varMetadata: labelDescription
##  | Scan Annotation:
## An object of class 'ScanAnnotationDataFrame'
##   scans: 1 2 ... 77 (77 total)
##   varLabels: scanID subjectID ... file (13 total)
##   varMetadata: labelDescription
#   Get the BAF/LRR data from a gds file

blfile <-
  system.file("extdata", "illumina_bl.gds", package = "GWASdata")
gds <- GdsIntensityReader(blfile)

#   Combine the BAF/LRR data with the sample and SNP annotation

blData <-
  IntensityData(gds, snpAnnot = snpAnnot, scanAnnot = scanAnnot)
blData
## An object of class IntensityData 
##  | data:
## File: /software/rhel9/manual/install/r/4.5.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_bl.gds (1.1M)
## +    [  ]
## |--+ sample.id   { Int32 77 ZIP(42.2%), 130B }
## |--+ snp.id   { Int32 3300 ZIP(38.5%), 5.0K }
## |--+ snp.chromosome   { UInt8 3300 ZIP(1.15%), 38B } *
## |--+ snp.position   { Int32 3300 ZIP(91.0%), 11.7K }
## |--+ snp.rs.id   { VStr8 3300 ZIP(38.4%), 12.5K }
## |--+ BAlleleFreq   { Float32 3300x77 ZIP(40.9%), 405.8K }
## \--+ LogRRatio   { Float32 3300x77 ZIP(68.2%), 676.6K }
##  | SNP Annotation:
## An object of class 'SnpAnnotationDataFrame'
##   snps: 1 2 ... 3300 (3300 total)
##   varLabels: snpID chromosome ... rBB (14 total)
##   varMetadata: labelDescription
##  | Scan Annotation:
## An object of class 'ScanAnnotationDataFrame'
##   scans: 1 2 ... 77 (77 total)
##   varLabels: scanID subjectID ... file (13 total)
##   varMetadata: labelDescription

Part 0 - Report

Using the functions introduced above to help you, briefly describe the dataset in a few sentences. Answer the following questions. Whatever code you use to help answer these questions can go in the empty chunk that follows.

  • What is the difference between the scanID and subjectID variables? Why are they both provided?

The scanID is the ID for each unique scan wile the subjectID is unique per subject. Both are provided because each subject may have multiple scans/samples that may be intended or unintended duplicates depending on the design of the study.

  • How many families are represented, and how many samples are from each family?

13

  • How many samples are of each sex?

F scans: 35 M scans: 42

  • How many samples are from each population group?

CEU: 49
YRI: 28

  • How many samples are there? How many people did they come from?

Samples: 77 People: 43

  • How many SNPs are there?

3300

  • Which chromosomes are represented? How many SNPs are on each?

21: 1000
22: 1000
23: 1000
24: 100
25: 100
26: 100

library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
## ✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
## ✔ purrr     1.1.0     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::combine()    masks Biobase::combine(), BiocGenerics::combine()
## ✖ dplyr::filter()     masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()        masks stats::lag()
## ✖ ggplot2::Position() masks BiocGenerics::Position(), base::Position()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
# What is the difference between the scanID and subjectID variables? Why are they both provided?
getMetadata(scanAnnot)
##                                                               labelDescription
## scanID                                                     unique ID for scans
## subjectID                         subject identifier (may have multiple scans)
## family                                                       family identifier
## father                                          father identifier as subjectID
## mother                                          mother identifier as subjectID
## CoriellID                                           Coriell subject identifier
## race                                                   HapMap population group
## sex                                           sex coded as M=male and F=female
## status                                           simulated case/control status
## genoRunID                                       genotyping instance identifier
## plate     plate containing samples processed together for genotyping chemistry
## batch                                               simulated genotyping batch
## file                                                             raw data file
scanAnnot %>%
  getAnnotation() %>%
  head()
##   scanID subjectID family    father    mother CoriellID race sex status
## 1    280 200191449   1341         0         0   NA06985  CEU   F      1
## 2    281 200191449   1341         0         0   NA06985  CEU   F      1
## 3    282 200030290   1341 200099417 200191449   NA06991  CEU   F      0
## 4    283 200030290   1341 200099417 200191449   NA06991  CEU   F      0
## 5    284 200099417   1341         0         0   NA06993  CEU   M      1
## 6    285 200099417   1341         0         0   NA06993  CEU   M      1
##                                genoRunID          plate batch
## 1 WG1000993-DNAG10-CIDR_06985@1007850397 WG0052814-AMP2     A
## 2 WG1000992-DNAF10-CIDR_06985@1007850586 WG0061258-AMP2     A
## 3 WG1000970-DNAB11-CIDR_06991@1007850444 WG0061536-AMP2     B
## 4 WG1000969-DNAA11-CIDR_06991@1007850587 WG0053489-AMP2     A
## 5 WG1000972-DNAE10-CIDR_06993@1007850591 WG0060475-AMP2     C
## 6 WG1000971-DNAD10-CIDR_06993@1007850421 WG0061540-AMP2     B
##                      file
## 1 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_37.csv
## 2 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_58.csv
## 3  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_5.csv
## 4  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_3.csv
## 5 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_10.csv
## 6 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_71.csv
# How many families are represented, and how many samples are from each family?
scanAnnot %>%
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  group_by(family) %>% 
  summarise(
    n = n()
  ) %>% 
  count
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##       n
##   <int>
## 1    13
# How many samples are of each sex?
n_scans <- scanAnnot %>%
      getAnnotation() %>%
      nrow()
n_F_scans <- scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(sex == 'F') %>% 
  nrow()
n_M_scans <- scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(sex == 'M') %>% 
  nrow()
print(
  paste0(
    "Total scans: ",
    n_scans
  )
)
## [1] "Total scans: 77"
print(
  paste0(
    "F scans: ",
    n_F_scans
  )
)
## [1] "F scans: 35"
print(
  paste0(
    "M scans: ",
    n_M_scans
  )
)
## [1] "M scans: 42"
# How many samples are from each population group?
scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  group_by(race) %>% 
  summarize(
    n = n()
  )
## # A tibble: 2 × 2
##   race      n
##   <chr> <int>
## 1 CEU      49
## 2 YRI      28
# How many samples are there? How many people did they come from?
scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  group_by(subjectID) %>% 
  summarize(
    n = n()
  ) %>% 
  count()
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##       n
##   <int>
## 1    43
# How many SNPs are there?
n_snp <- snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  nrow()
print(
  paste0(
    "There are ",
    n_snp,
    " SNPs."
  )
)
## [1] "There are 3300 SNPs."
# Which chromosomes are represented? How many SNPs are on each?
print("For all calls:")
## [1] "For all calls:"
snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  select(chromosome, snpID) %>% 
  unique() %>% 
  group_by(chromosome) %>% 
  summarize(
    n = n()
  )
## # A tibble: 6 × 2
##   chromosome     n
##        <int> <int>
## 1         21  1000
## 2         22  1000
## 3         23  1000
## 4         24   100
## 5         25   100
## 6         26   100

Part 1 - Missing Call Rate for Samples and SNPs

The basic {GWASTools} functions for missingness are missingGenotypeBySnpSex() and missingGenotypeByScanChrom(). missingGenotypeBySnpSex() returns a list of three objects: missing.counts, scans.per.sex, and missing.fraction. Read the help file for this function so that you understand what information is contained in these and how the Y chromosome is treated by sex (where sex is as defined in the scan annotation). Similarly, missingGenotypeByScanChrom() returns missing.counts, snps.per.chr, and missing.fraction.

There are four basic missingness calculations to be done, in the following order.

  1. Calculate missing.n1 = fraction of genotype calls missing over all samples (except that annotated ‘female’ individuals are excluded for Y chr SNPs)

  2. Calculate missing.e1 = missing call rate per sample over all SNPs (excluding SNPs with all calls missing)

  3. Calculate missing.n2 = missing call rate per SNP over all samples whose missing.e1 is less than 0.05

  4. Calculate missing.e2 = missing call rate per sample over all SNPs with missing.n2 values less than 0.05

We’ll want to store the missingness information in the annotation data frames as we proceed.

Here’s how to calculate and store missing.n1.

### Using missingGenotypeBySnpSex to calculate missing.n1 ###

#   First look at some of the results

miss <- missingGenotypeBySnpSex(genoData)
names(miss)
## [1] "missing.counts"   "scans.per.sex"    "missing.fraction"
head(miss$missing.counts)
##         M  F
## 999447 42 35
## 999465  0  1
## 999493  3  2
## 999512  0  0
## 999561  0  0
## 999567  0  0
miss$scans.per.sex
##  M  F 
## 42 35
head(miss$missing.fraction)
##     999447     999465     999493     999512     999561     999567 
## 1.00000000 0.01298701 0.06493506 0.00000000 0.00000000 0.00000000
#   We should make sure the snpIDs are in the same order in the annotation and
#   in the missingness report (miss$missing.fraction); they are

allequal(snpAnnot$snpID, as.numeric(names(miss$missing.fraction)))
## [1] TRUE
#   Then go ahead and add missing.n1 as a new annotation data column, and update
#   the metadata

snpAnnot$missing.n1 <- miss$missing.fraction
varMetadata(snpAnnot)["missing.n1", "labelDescription"] <-
  paste(
    "fraction of genotype calls missing over all samples, except that females are excluded for Y chr SNPs"
  )

### Explore the missing call rate per probes a little more

#   Look at the distribution of missingness for all the probes

hist(
  snpAnnot$missing.n1,
  ylim = c(0, 100),
  xlab = "SNP missing call rate",
  main = "Missing Call Rate for All Probes"
)

#   How many SNPs are missing a genotype for every single sample? Apparently
#   there are 151 such SNPs

sum(snpAnnot$missing.n1 == 1)
## [1] 151
#   Store a vector containing the snpIDs of all the SNPs with missingness of
#   100%

snpexcl <- snpAnnot$snpID[snpAnnot$missing.n1 == 1]

Now we calculate and store missing.e1. The steps are described in the comments below.

### Using missingGenotypeByScanChrom to calculate missing.e1

#   Apply missingGenotypeByScanChrom to genoData, using the snp.exclude option
#   to exclude these SNPs (why does it make sense to exclude those SNPs?)

miss <- missingGenotypeByScanChrom(genoData, snp.exclude = snpexcl)

#   Make sure the order of the scanIDs in the output from
#   missingGenotypeByScanChrom is the same as the order of the scanIDs in the
#   annotation data frame

allequal(names(miss$missing.fraction), scanAnnot$scanID)
## [1] TRUE
#   Make a new column in scanAnnot called missing.e1 and store the per-scan
#   missingness from missingGenotypeByScanChrom there

scanAnnot$missing.e1 <- miss$missing.fraction

#   Update the scanAnnot metadata

varMetadata(scanAnnot)["missing.e1", "labelDescription"] <-
  "fraction of genotype calls missing over all snps with missing.n1<1 except that Y chr SNPs are excluded for females"

# We can check summary statistics and make a histogram, too

summary(scanAnnot$missing.e1)
##      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
## 0.0000000 0.0009656 0.0022229 0.0032162 0.0041283 0.0247827
hist(
  scanAnnot$missing.e1,
  xlab = "Fraction of missing calls over all probes",
  main = "Histogram of Sample Missing Call Rate for All Samples"
)

print("For only calls MCR < 1:")
## [1] "For only calls MCR < 1:"
snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.n1 < 1) %>% 
  select(chromosome, snpID) %>% 
  unique() %>% 
  group_by(chromosome) %>% 
  summarize(
    n = n()
  )
## # A tibble: 6 × 2
##   chromosome     n
##        <int> <int>
## 1         21   999
## 2         22   997
## 3         23   950
## 4         24    61
## 5         25    42
## 6         26   100

Now, supply your code to calculate and store missing.n2. The steps you should follow are described in the comments below.

### Now that missing.n1 and missing.e1 have been calculated, we can calculate missing.n2

#   Recall that missing.n2 = call rate per SNP over all samples whose missing.e1
#   is less than 0.05.

#   Since no sample had missing.e1 > 0.05 in this dataset, we don't actually
#   have to exclude any samples.

#   However, let's proceed as if there were some samples with missing.e1 > 0.05.

#   Make a vector of the scanIDs for the samples with high missing.e1.

snp_call_rate_gt_0_05 <- scanAnnot$scanID[scanAnnot$missing.e1 > 0.05]

#   Apply the missingGenotypeBySnpSex function again, using the scan.exclude
#   argument.

miss <- missingGenotypeBySnpSex(genoData, scan.exclude = snp_call_rate_gt_0_05)

#   Store missing.n2 as a new column in snpAnnot.

snpAnnot$missing.n2 <- miss$missing.fraction

#   Update the metadata for snpAnnot!

#   The labelDescription for missing.n2 should be "fraction of genotype calls
#   missing over all samples with missing.e1 < 0.05 except that females are
#   excluded for Y chr SNPs"

varMetadata(snpAnnot)["missing.n2", "labelDescription"] <-
  "fraction of genotype calls missing over all samples with missing.e1 < 0.05 except that females are excluded for Y chr SNPs"

summary(snpAnnot$missing.n2)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
## 0.00000 0.00000 0.00000 0.04898 0.00000 1.00000
hist(
  snpAnnot$missing.n2,
  xlab = "Fraction of missing calls over all samples w/MCR < 0.05",
  main = "Histogram of SNPs Missing Call Rate for All Samples w/MCR < 0.05"
)

Write code to calculate and store missing.e2. The steps you should follow are described in the comments below.

### Last, we calculate missing.e2

#   Recall missing.e2 = missing call rate per sample over all SNPs with
#   missing.n2 values less than 0.05

#   Store a vector with the snpIDs of all probes with missing.n2 >= 0.05

sample_call_rate_gte_0_05 <- snpAnnot$snpID[snpAnnot$missing.n2 >= 0.05]

#   Apply missingGenotypeByScanChrom to genoData again, using the snp.exclude
#   argument to exclude the SNPs with missing.n2 >= 0.05 (why do we do this?)

miss <- missingGenotypeByScanChrom(genoData, snp.exclude = sample_call_rate_gte_0_05)

#   Make a new column in scanAnnot called missing.e2 to store the missingness
#   per-scan you just calculated

scanAnnot$missing.e2 <- miss$missing.fraction

#   Update the scanAnnot metadata

#   The labelDescription should say "fraction of genotype calls missing over all
#   snps with missing.n2 < 0.05 except that Y chr SNPs are excluded for females"

varMetadata(scanAnnot)["missing.e2", "labelDescription"] <-
  "fraction of genotype calls missing over all snps with missing.n2 < 0.05 except that Y chr SNPs are excluded for females"

hist(
  scanAnnot$missing.e2,
  xlab = "Fraction of missing calls over all snps w/MCR < 0.05",
  main = "Histogram of Sample Missing Call Rate for All SNPs w/MCR < 0.05"
)

Part 1 - Report

Summarize the filtering you’ve accomplished at this step. Include the following in your answer the following questions.

  • (First, make sure you’ve filled in the calculations of missing.n2 and missing.e2 in the chunks above.)

  • How many SNPs and samples were genotyped?

  • Briefly describe the purpose of doing this step in two rounds (why do we calculate both missingnesses twice?)

  • What were the median, mean, and maximum per-sample missingness (use missing.e2)? Make a histogram of missing.e2.

  • How many samples had no missing calls at all (use missing.e2)?

  • Are the samples generally “good”? How many should be excluded from downstream analyses?

  • How many SNPs had a 100% missing call rate (use missing.n1)?

  • What are the median, mean, and maximum per-SNP missingnesses (use missing.n2)? How many SNPs are missing no calls at all?

  • Make a histogram of missing.n2.

  • How many SNPs are filtered out and how many remain if you retain only SNPs with missing.n2 < 0.05?

  • Make a histogram showing missing.n2 for the remaining SNPs.

# How many SNPs and samples were genotyped?

print("Number of SNPs attempted to be genotyped:")
## [1] "Number of SNPs attempted to be genotyped:"
nrow(snpAnnot)
## snps 
## 3300
print("Number of SNPs genotyped:")
## [1] "Number of SNPs genotyped:"
snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.n1 < 1) %>% 
  nrow()
## [1] 3149
print("Number of Samples attempted to be genotyped:")
## [1] "Number of Samples attempted to be genotyped:"
nrow(scanAnnot)
## scans 
##    77
print("Number of Samples genotyped:")
## [1] "Number of Samples genotyped:"
scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.e1 < 1) %>%
  nrow()
## [1] 77
# Briefly describe the purpose of doing this step in two rounds (why do we calculate both missingnesses twice?)

print("A: We undergo two rounds of missingness alternating between sample-wise and snp-wise filtering.")
## [1] "A: We undergo two rounds of missingness alternating between sample-wise and snp-wise filtering."
print("The first round is to filter based on overall probe-wise (per probe for all samples) and sample-wise (per sample across all probes) missingness.")
## [1] "The first round is to filter based on overall probe-wise (per probe for all samples) and sample-wise (per sample across all probes) missingness."
print("The second round repeats the task with a limit of missingess less than 5%.")
## [1] "The second round repeats the task with a limit of missingess less than 5%."
print("Probe-wise for round 2 ensures we have only probes from high quality samples.")
## [1] "Probe-wise for round 2 ensures we have only probes from high quality samples."
print("Sample-wise for round 2 ensures we have only samples from high quality probes.")
## [1] "Sample-wise for round 2 ensures we have only samples from high quality probes."
# What were the median, mean, and maximum per-sample missingness (use `missing.e2`)?

print("See table and histogram for per-sample missingness overview...")
## [1] "See table and histogram for per-sample missingness overview..."
scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  summarise(
    median = median(missing.e2),
    mean = mean(missing.e2),
    max = max(missing.e2)
  )
##         median        mean        max
## 1 0.0006544503 0.001828865 0.02421466
# Make a histogram of `missing.e2`.

hist(
  scanAnnot$missing.e2,
  xlab = "Missing Call Rate",
  main = "Frequency of Sample Missingness in High Quality SNPs"
)

# How many samples had no missing calls at all (use `missing.e2`)?

print("Samples with no missing calls:")
## [1] "Samples with no missing calls:"
scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.e2 == 0) %>% 
  nrow()
## [1] 6
# Are the samples generally "good"? How many should be excluded from downstream analyses?

print("Overall samples to be excluded:")
## [1] "Overall samples to be excluded:"
scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.e2 > 0.05) %>% 
  nrow()
## [1] 0
print("Overall, it was found that the samples were high quality and none were excluded from downstream analysis.")
## [1] "Overall, it was found that the samples were high quality and none were excluded from downstream analysis."
# How many SNPs had a 100% missing call rate (use `missing.n1`)?

print("Number of SNPs with 100% MCR:")
## [1] "Number of SNPs with 100% MCR:"
n_100_perc_missing_snps <- snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.n1 == 1) %>% 
  nrow()
n_100_perc_missing_snps
## [1] 151
# 151 ... now I have to add a pokemon easter egg for the original 151
# ...placed at the bottom of this report

# What are the median, mean, and maximum per-SNP missingnesses (use `missing.n2`)? How many SNPs are missing no calls at all?

print("See table and histogram for per-SNP missingness overview...")
## [1] "See table and histogram for per-SNP missingness overview..."
snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  summarise(
    median = median(missing.n2),
    mean = mean(missing.n2),
    max = max(missing.n2)
  )
##   median       mean max
## 1      0 0.04898465   1
# Make a histogram of `missing.n2`.

hist(
  snpAnnot$missing.n2,
  xlab = "Missing Call Rate",
  main = "Frequency of SNP Missingness in High Quality Samples"
)

# How many SNPs are filtered out and how many remain if you retain only SNPs with `missing.n2` \< 0.05?

print("Remaining SNPs if only retaining SNPs with MCR < 0.05")
## [1] "Remaining SNPs if only retaining SNPs with MCR < 0.05"
retained_snps <- snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.n2 < 0.05)
retained_snps %>% nrow()
## [1] 3095
# Make a histogram showing `missing.n2` for the remaining SNPs.
retained_snps$missing.n2 %>% 
  hist(
    xlab = "Missing Call Rate",
    main = "Frequency of SNP Missingness in High Quality Samples w/MCR < 0.05"
  )

retained_scans <- scanAnnot %>%
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.e2 < 0.05)

# How many families are represented, and how many samples are from each family?
retained_scans %>% 
  group_by(family) %>% 
  summarise(
    n = n()
  ) %>% 
  count
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##       n
##   <int>
## 1    13
# How many samples are of each sex?
n_scans <- retained_scans %>%
      nrow()
n_F_scans <- retained_scans %>% 
  filter(sex == 'F') %>% 
  nrow()
n_M_scans <- retained_scans %>% 
  filter(sex == 'M') %>% 
  nrow()
print(
  paste0(
    "Total scans: ",
    n_scans
  )
)
## [1] "Total scans: 77"
print(
  paste0(
    "F scans: ",
    n_F_scans
  )
)
## [1] "F scans: 35"
print(
  paste0(
    "M scans: ",
    n_M_scans
  )
)
## [1] "M scans: 42"
# How many samples are from each population group?
retained_scans %>% 
  group_by(race) %>% 
  summarize(
    n = n()
  )
## # A tibble: 2 × 2
##   race      n
##   <chr> <int>
## 1 CEU      49
## 2 YRI      28
# How many samples are there? How many people did they come from?
retained_scans %>% 
  group_by(subjectID) %>% 
  summarize(
    n = n()
  ) %>% 
  count()
## # A tibble: 1 × 1
##       n
##   <int>
## 1    43
retained_snps %>% nrow()
## [1] 3095
retained_snps %>% 
  select(chromosome, snpID) %>% 
  unique() %>% 
  group_by(chromosome) %>% 
  summarize(
    n = n()
  )
## # A tibble: 6 × 2
##   chromosome     n
##        <int> <int>
## 1         21   985
## 2         22   976
## 3         23   950
## 4         24    58
## 5         25    39
## 6         26    87

Part 2 - Inferred vs. Reported Sex and Relatedness

To investigate discrepancies in inferred vs. reported sex (and, possibly, sex chromosome aneuploidies), we will produce four plots that summarize each scan’s heterozygosity and intensity for the sex chromosomes:

  1. Mean Y chromosome vs. mean X chromosome intensity

  2. Mean X chromosome heterozygosity vs. mean X chromosome intensity

  3. Mean Y chromosome intensity vs. mean X chromosome heterozygosity

  4. Mean X chromosome heterozygosity vs. autosomal heterozygosity

Recall that the intensity data was read in earlier as qxyData. Before making our graphs, we’ll need to calculate the variables we want to plot. The basic function we’ll use to do this is meanIntensityByScanChrom(), which will return a list of matrices. Read the help file of this function so that you understand the output. Heterozygosity is calculated with hetByScanChrom().

#   Look at the structure of the IntensityData object

qxyData
## An object of class IntensityData 
##  | data:
## File: /software/rhel9/manual/install/r/4.5.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_qxy.gds (1.0M)
## +    [  ]
## |--+ sample.id   { Int32 77 ZIP(42.2%), 130B }
## |--+ snp.id   { Int32 3300 ZIP(38.5%), 5.0K }
## |--+ snp.chromosome   { UInt8 3300 ZIP(1.15%), 38B } *
## |--+ snp.position   { Int32 3300 ZIP(91.0%), 11.7K }
## |--+ snp.rs.id   { VStr8 3300 ZIP(38.4%), 12.5K }
## |--+ quality   { Float32 3300x77 ZIP(4.93%), 48.9K }
## |--+ X   { Float32 3300x77 ZIP(50.3%), 498.8K }
## \--+ Y   { Float32 3300x77 ZIP(49.4%), 490.5K }
##  | SNP Annotation:
## An object of class 'SnpAnnotationDataFrame'
##   snps: 1 2 ... 3300 (3300 total)
##   varLabels: snpID chromosome ... rBB (14 total)
##   varMetadata: labelDescription
##  | Scan Annotation:
## An object of class 'ScanAnnotationDataFrame'
##   scans: 1 2 ... 77 (77 total)
##   varLabels: scanID subjectID ... file (13 total)
##   varMetadata: labelDescription
#   For each probe, there's a numerical ID, chromosome, position, rsID

#   The intensities (stored as variables called X and Y) are each essentially
#   3300×77 matrices (an X and Y intensity for each scan for every single probe)

#   (Don't confuse the *intensity variable names* _X_ and _Y_ in the IntensityData
#   object with the X and Y *chromosomes*!)

#   Apply the meanIntensityByScanChrom function to get the intensity averages.
#   (We're not going to use all of the output from this function)

inten.by.chrom <- meanIntensityByScanChrom(qxyData)

#   Extract the matrix containing the mean intensity for each chromosome for
#   each scan

names(inten.by.chrom)
## [1] "mean.intensity" "sd.intensity"   "mean.X"         "sd.X"          
## [5] "mean.Y"         "sd.Y"
mninten <- inten.by.chrom$mean.intensity

#   The matrix is 77x6 since there are 77 samples and 6 chromosomes represented
#   in the dataset

dim(mninten)
## [1] 77  6
head(mninten)
##           21       22        X      XY         Y        M
## 280 1.145446 1.393316 1.118105 1.10446 0.2830108  3.54744
## 281 1.145529 1.378407 1.128745 1.09263 0.2774100  2.41541
## 282 1.139627 1.364754 1.122555 1.10036 0.3043980  4.55299
## 283 1.181063 1.475676 1.136374 1.16277 0.2789200 10.84578
## 284 1.177230 1.436911 0.730352 1.16742 0.9116500  3.38423
## 285 1.120587 1.387991 0.704766 1.09283 0.8304700  3.04392
#   We also need to calculate the X chromosome and autosome heterozygosities for
#   each sample before we can plot them

#   Apply hetByScanChrom to genoData (we need actual genotypes to calculate the
#   heterozygosity)

het.results <- hetByScanChrom(genoData)

#   The function calculates eachs scan's heterozygosity for each chromosome, and
#   also for the autosomes as a whole

#   We want to add X and autosome heterozygosity as new columns in scanAnnot

#   Make sure the scanIDs are in the right order first! (they are)

allequal(scanAnnot$scanID, rownames(het.results))
## [1] TRUE
#   Extract the autosomal and X heterozygosity and store them in scanAnnot

scanAnnot$het.A <- het.results[, "A"]
scanAnnot$het.X <- het.results[, "X"]

#   Add appropriate metadata

varMetadata(scanAnnot)["het.A", "labelDescription"] <-
  "fraction of heterozygotes for autosomal SNPs"
varMetadata(scanAnnot)["het.X", "labelDescription"] <-
  "fraction of heterozygotes for X chromosome SNPs"

#   Before making the plots, there are a few things to do

#   We want to color the plotted points by annotated sex

#   But first, make sure that the order of the scanIDs (and hence the annotated
#   sexes) matches the order of the scanIDs in the data we'll plot

allequal(scanAnnot$scanID, rownames(mninten))
## [1] TRUE
#   Now we assign each sex a color (male=blue, female=red)

#   Store the colors in a vector

xcol <- rep(NA, nrow(scanAnnot))
xcol[scanAnnot$sex == "M"] <- "blue"
xcol[scanAnnot$sex == "F"] <- "red"

#   We also want to count the number of SNPs getting included on the X and Y
#   chromosomes (which are coded as 23 and 25 in snpAnnot)

nx <- sum(snpAnnot$chromosome == 23)
ny <- sum(snpAnnot$chromosome == 25)

# Now make the plots

# All intensities

x1 <- mninten[, "X"]
y1 <- mninten[, "Y"]
main1 <- "Mean X vs \nMean Y Chromosome Intensity"

# Het on X vs X intensity

x2 <- mninten[, "X"]
y2 <- scanAnnot$het.X
main2 <-
  "Mean X Chromosome Intensity vs Mean X Chromosome Heterozygosity"

# Het on X vs Y intensity

y3 <- mninten[, "Y"]
x3 <- scanAnnot$het.X
main3 <-
  "MeanX Chromosome Heterozygosity vs Mean Y Chromosome Intensity"

# X vs A het

x4 <- scanAnnot$het.A[scanAnnot$sex == "F"]
y4 <- scanAnnot$het.X[scanAnnot$sex == "F"]
main4 <-
  "Mean Autosomal Heterozygosity vs Mean X Chromosome Heterozygosity"

# Make labels for axes/legends

cols <- c("blue", "red")
mf <- c("male", "female")
xintenlab <- paste("X intensity (n=", nx, ")", sep = "")
yintenlab <- paste("Y intensity (n=", ny, ")", sep = "")

# Make the 4 plots and add a legend

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(
  x1,
  y1,
  xlab = xintenlab,
  ylab = yintenlab,
  main = main1,
  col = xcol,
  cex.main = 0.8
)
legend("bottomleft",
       mf,
       col = cols,
       pch = c(1, 1),
       cex = 0.5)
plot(
  x2,
  y2,
  col = xcol,
  xlab = xintenlab,
  ylab = "X heterozygosity",
  main = main2,
  cex.main = 0.8
)
plot(
  x3,
  y3,
  col = xcol,
  ylab = yintenlab,
  xlab = "X heterozygosity",
  main = main3,
  cex.main = 0.8
)
plot(
  x4,
  y4,
  col = "red",
  xlab = "Autosomal heterozygosity",
  ylab = "X heterozygosity",
  main = main4,
  cex.main = 0.8
)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Part 2 - Report (Sex Check)

  • Describe any anomalies you see in the 4 plots above.

  • Figure out the scanIDs of any scans that look like outliers or possible instances of mislabeled sex. Include these IDs in your report and describe why they appear to be mislabeled.

  • For the purposes of this assignment we will ASSUME that some scans have just had their sexes mislabeled. (In “real life”, we would not just assume this, and we might instead flag these scans for removal from some downstream steps/analyses.)

  • Manually change the sex annotation for these scans in scanAnnot to “remove” the discrepancy. (Manually changing your data is risky and not generally recommended. Be careful!). In your report, state exactly what changes you made. Add a new column to scanAnnot indicating this, too (add appropriate metadata). Does anything in the pedigree structure make you more confident that this “fix” is correct? (Are these samples labeled as mothers or fathers?)

  • Remake the 4 plots above, using the “corrected” data (nothing should appear mislabeled now for this dataset).

  • How might you follow up on this “in real life”?

# Describe any anomalies you see in the 4 plots above.

print("There appear to be 2 or 3 reported females that are inferring XY.")
## [1] "There appear to be 2 or 3 reported females that are inferring XY."
print("These can be seen in the X vs Y intensity and heteroygosity plots and the X intensity vs X heterozygosity plots.")
## [1] "These can be seen in the X vs Y intensity and heteroygosity plots and the X intensity vs X heterozygosity plots."
print("There also appear to be some outliers with low X heterogeneity vs autosomal heteroygosity.")
## [1] "There also appear to be some outliers with low X heterogeneity vs autosomal heteroygosity."
print("These are likely the reported females that we can infer are male.")
## [1] "These are likely the reported females that we can infer are male."
# Figure out the scanIDs of any scans that look like outliers or possible instances of mislabeled sex. Include these IDs in your report and describe why they appear to be mislabeled.

# get the outliers from the reported females with a low X heterozygosity (arbitrarily chose 0.1 give the graphs)
diff_inferred_sex <- scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(sex == "F" & het.X < 0.1) %>%
  select(scanID) %>%
  unlist()

print('Scans that appear to have mislabeled sex (in "incorrect:" cluster):')
## [1] "Scans that appear to have mislabeled sex (in \"incorrect:\" cluster):"
diff_inferred_sex
## scanID1 scanID2 
##     325     326
# For the purposes of this assignment we will ASSUME that some scans have just had their sexes mislabeled. (In "real life", we would not just assume this, and we might instead flag these scans for removal from some downstream steps/analyses.)

# Manually change the sex annotation for these scans in scanAnnot to "remove" the discrepancy. (Manually changing your data is risky and not generally recommended. Be careful!). In your report, state exactly what changes you made. Add a new column to scanAnnot indicating this, too (add appropriate metadata). Does anything in the pedigree structure make you more confident that this "fix" is correct? (Are these samples labeled as mothers or fathers?)

# save the reported sex values
scanAnnot$reported_sex <- scanAnnot$sex
# update the descriptions of sex and reported_sex
varMetadata(scanAnnot)["sex", "labelDescription"] <-
  "sex coded as M=male and F=female as corrected from inference from the data"
varMetadata(scanAnnot)["reported_sex", "labelDescription"] <-
  "sex coded as M=male and F=female as reported in the raw data"

# manually update the sex given the scanIDs identified
updated_sex <- scanAnnot$sex
print("Before reported/inferred sex correction:")
## [1] "Before reported/inferred sex correction:"
table(updated_sex)
## updated_sex
##  F  M 
## 35 42
names(updated_sex) <- scanAnnot$scanID

for(scan in diff_inferred_sex) {
  idx <- toString(scan)
  updated_sex[idx] <- if_else(updated_sex[idx] == "F", "M", "F")
}

print("After reported/inferred sex correction:")
## [1] "After reported/inferred sex correction:"
table(updated_sex)
## updated_sex
##  F  M 
## 33 44
scanAnnot$sex <- updated_sex

# Remake the 4 plots above, using the "corrected" data (nothing should appear mislabeled now for this dataset).

# can just copy & paste the graphing code above because we updated the "sex" column itself...
# --- start copied code ---

#   Apply hetByScanChrom to genoData (we need actual genotypes to calculate the
#   heterozygosity)

het.results <- hetByScanChrom(genoData)

#   The function calculates eachs scan's heterozygosity for each chromosome, and
#   also for the autosomes as a whole

#   We want to add X and autosome heterozygosity as new columns in scanAnnot

#   Make sure the scanIDs are in the right order first! (they are)

allequal(scanAnnot$scanID, rownames(het.results))
## [1] TRUE
#   Extract the autosomal and X heterozygosity and store them in scanAnnot

scanAnnot$het.A <- het.results[, "A"]
scanAnnot$het.X <- het.results[, "X"]

#   Add appropriate metadata

varMetadata(scanAnnot)["het.A", "labelDescription"] <-
  "fraction of heterozygotes for autosomal SNPs"
varMetadata(scanAnnot)["het.X", "labelDescription"] <-
  "fraction of heterozygotes for X chromosome SNPs"

#   Before making the plots, there are a few things to do

#   We want to color the plotted points by annotated sex

#   But first, make sure that the order of the scanIDs (and hence the annotated
#   sexes) matches the order of the scanIDs in the data we'll plot

allequal(scanAnnot$scanID, rownames(mninten))
## [1] TRUE
#   Now we assign each sex a color (male=blue, female=red)

#   Store the colors in a vector

xcol <- rep(NA, nrow(scanAnnot))
xcol[scanAnnot$sex == "M"] <- "blue"
xcol[scanAnnot$sex == "F"] <- "red"

#   We also want to count the number of SNPs getting included on the X and Y
#   chromosomes (which are coded as 23 and 25 in snpAnnot)

nx <- sum(snpAnnot$chromosome == 23)
ny <- sum(snpAnnot$chromosome == 25)

# Now make the plots

# All intensities

x1 <- mninten[, "X"]
y1 <- mninten[, "Y"]
main1 <- "Mean X vs \nMean Y Chromosome Intensity"

# Het on X vs X intensity

x2 <- mninten[, "X"]
y2 <- scanAnnot$het.X
main2 <-
  "Mean X Chromosome Intensity vs Mean X Chromosome Heterozygosity"

# Het on X vs Y intensity

y3 <- mninten[, "Y"]
x3 <- scanAnnot$het.X
main3 <-
  "MeanX Chromosome Heterozygosity vs Mean Y Chromosome Intensity"

# X vs A het

x4 <- scanAnnot$het.A[scanAnnot$sex == "F"]
y4 <- scanAnnot$het.X[scanAnnot$sex == "F"]
main4 <-
  "Mean Autosomal Heterozygosity vs Mean X Chromosome Heterozygosity"

# Make labels for axes/legends

cols <- c("blue", "red")
mf <- c("male", "female")
xintenlab <- paste("X intensity (n=", nx, ")", sep = "")
yintenlab <- paste("Y intensity (n=", ny, ")", sep = "")

# Make the 4 plots and add a legend

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(
  x1,
  y1,
  xlab = xintenlab,
  ylab = yintenlab,
  main = main1,
  col = xcol,
  cex.main = 0.8
)
legend("bottomleft",
       mf,
       col = cols,
       pch = c(1, 1),
       cex = 0.5)
plot(
  x2,
  y2,
  col = xcol,
  xlab = xintenlab,
  ylab = "X heterozygosity",
  main = main2,
  cex.main = 0.8
)
plot(
  x3,
  y3,
  col = xcol,
  ylab = yintenlab,
  xlab = "X heterozygosity",
  main = main3,
  cex.main = 0.8
)
plot(
  x4,
  y4,
  col = "red",
  xlab = "Autosomal heterozygosity",
  ylab = "X heterozygosity",
  main = main4,
  cex.main = 0.8
)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

# --- end copied code ---

# How might you follow up on this "in real life"?

print("I would check with any RAs to see if any surveys, general collection of the data, or the individual themselves may have specified a different gender identity than biological sex or if they maybe mislabeled the sex.")
## [1] "I would check with any RAs to see if any surveys, general collection of the data, or the individual themselves may have specified a different gender identity than biological sex or if they maybe mislabeled the sex."
print('If unable to confirm these inferences, I would consult the PI on if they want to keep these "corrected" data or drop these samples.')
## [1] "If unable to confirm these inferences, I would consult the PI on if they want to keep these \"corrected\" data or drop these samples."
scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(scanID %in% diff_inferred_sex)
##   scanID subjectID family father mother CoriellID race sex status
## 1    325 200094287   1408      0      0   NA12155  CEU   M      1
## 2    326 200094287   1408      0      0   NA12155  CEU   M      1
##                                genoRunID          plate batch
## 1 WG1000973-DNAD07-CIDR_12155@1007850404 WG0053513-AMP2     C
## 2 WG1000974-DNAE07-CIDR_12155@1007850413 WG0053492-AMP2     B
##                      file   missing.e1   missing.e2     het.A het.X
## 1 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_13.csv 0.0016092694 0.0009816754 0.2814852     0
## 2 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_17.csv 0.0003218539 0.0003272251 0.2812030     0
##   reported_sex
## 1            F
## 2            F

Part 3 - Allelic Imbalance; BAF and LRR Plots

Here we want to look at B allele frequency (BAF) and log R ratio (LRR) plots using chromIntensityPlot(). Ordinarily, since there are 22 to 23 chromosomes to check for every sample, you would use a script that automatically detects aberrations in BAF and LRR. For this project, we’ll pretend that our pipeline has already flagged one sample for a possible chromosomal anomaly.

# Recall that earlier we read the intensity data into an IntensityData object
# called blData.

# The snp/scan annotation were attached as well.

# Suppose that chromosome 22 was flagged for scanID 286.

# Making the BAF/LRR plot is easy

#dev.off()
chromIntensityPlot(blData, scan.ids = 286, chrom.ids = 22)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Part 3 - Report

  • Describe the purpose of this step.

  • Show an example of a “normal” pair of BAF/LRR plots (choose any chromosome/scanID you want).

  • How would you detect trisomy 21 (i.e., describe briefly in words what the BAF/LRR plot would look like).

  • Suppose an individual had a balanced reciprocal translocation involving chromosome 2 and 4. What would you expect that individual’s BAF/LRR plots would look like for those chromosomes?

  • Suppose chromosome 22 in sample 286 was the only chromosome flagged for a possible anomaly in this dataset. Looking at the plot, do you notice anything strange? Can you suggest what kind of anomaly might be showing up? (There isn’t any single answer that’s certainly right! The purpose of this question is to check that you understand what the BAF and LRR plots “mean”).

  • Finally, suppose your pipeline has given you a list of the exact chromosomal segments showing possibly aneuploidies/anomalies in each sample. What would you recommend doing with the genotypes in those segments? (You’re not being asked to implement this here - just say what you recommend doing.)

# Describe the purpose of this step.

print("The purpose of this step is to check for a number of potential anomalies including:")
## [1] "The purpose of this step is to check for a number of potential anomalies including:"
print("- segments of chromosomal deletions, duplicatons, and runs of homozygosity")
## [1] "- segments of chromosomal deletions, duplicatons, and runs of homozygosity"
print("- potential aneuploidies")
## [1] "- potential aneuploidies"
print("- admixture, whole genome mosaicism, or chimerism")
## [1] "- admixture, whole genome mosaicism, or chimerism"
print("Ultimitely, we are looking to genotypes to exclude or whole samples whose copy number is unexpected.")
## [1] "Ultimitely, we are looking to genotypes to exclude or whole samples whose copy number is unexpected."
# Show an example of a "normal" pair of BAF/LRR plots (choose any chromosome/scanID you want).

chromIntensityPlot(blData, scan.ids = 284, chrom.ids = 21)

# How would you detect trisomy 21 (i.e., describe briefly in words what the BAF/LRR plot would look like).

print("I would expect the BAF plot to have 4 distinct bands around 0, 0.333, 0.667, and 1.")
## [1] "I would expect the BAF plot to have 4 distinct bands around 0, 0.333, 0.667, and 1."
print("I would expect the band of the LRR plot to be upshifted form 0 to around 0.5 or higher.")
## [1] "I would expect the band of the LRR plot to be upshifted form 0 to around 0.5 or higher."
# Suppose an individual had a balanced reciprocal translocation involving chromosome 2 and 4. What would you expect that individual's BAF/LRR plots would look like for those chromosomes?

print("I would expect them to still appear normal. The balanced and recipricol nature of them makes the amount of genetic material and karyotypes unchanged, so I woulnd't expect to see any anomolies in the plots related to this.")
## [1] "I would expect them to still appear normal. The balanced and recipricol nature of them makes the amount of genetic material and karyotypes unchanged, so I woulnd't expect to see any anomolies in the plots related to this."
# Suppose chromosome 22 in sample 286 was the only chromosome flagged for a possible anomaly in this dataset. Looking at the plot, do you notice anything strange? Can you suggest what kind of anomaly might be showing up? (There isn't any single answer that's certainly right! The purpose of this question is to check that you understand what the BAF and LRR plots "mean").

print("It looks mostly fine. There may some signs for deletions and/or runs of homozygosity.")
## [1] "It looks mostly fine. There may some signs for deletions and/or runs of homozygosity."
# Finally, suppose your pipeline has given you a list of the exact chromosomal segments showing possibly aneuploidies/anomalies in each sample. What would you recommend doing with the genotypes in those segments? (You're not being asked to implement this here - just say what you recommend doing.)

print("Quite simply, I would recommend removing those genotypes from those chromosomal segments.")
## [1] "Quite simply, I would recommend removing those genotypes from those chromosomal segments."

Easter Egg - Uncalled SNP Pokedex

# references stackoverflow for this: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/73535956/how-to-insert-an-image-from-a-url-into-r-markdown-html-vignette
# and this:
# https://r-graph-gallery.com/363-add-images-and-links-in-kableExtra.html
get_pokemon_url <- function(number) {
  knitr::include_graphics(
    paste0(
      "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/PokeAPI/sprites/refs/heads/master/sprites/pokemon/",
      number,
      ".png"
    )
  )
}

print("Generating uncalled SNP Pokedex...")
## [1] "Generating uncalled SNP Pokedex..."
library(kableExtra)
## 
## Attaching package: 'kableExtra'
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     group_rows
uncalled_snps <- snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.n1 == 1) %>%
  mutate(
    id = row_number()
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    pokemon = get_pokemon_url(id)
  ) %>% 
  select(id, rsID, chromosome, pokemon)
pokemon <- uncalled_snps$pokemon
uncalled_snps %>% 
  mutate(pokemon = "") %>% 
  kbl(booktabs = T, align = "c") %>%
  kable_styling() %>%
  kable_paper(full_width = T) %>%
  column_spec(
    4,
    image = spec_image(pokemon, 280, 200) # dimensions of the images
  )
id rsID chromosome pokemon
1 rs3132407 21
2 rs3996123 22
3 rs79288 22
4 rs5749211 22
5 rs5989512 23
6 rs5979499 23

10 rs16980777 23
11 rs16983251 23

13 rs5917576 23
14 rs5917261 23
15 rs17145269 23
16 rs207054 23
17 rs1571441 23

20 rs2342835 23

22 rs16981346 23
23 rs5941160 23
24 rs2534283 23
25 rs4892997 23
26 rs2808741 23
27 rs7882088 23
28 rs241852 23
29 rs4907813 23
30 rs11798470 23
31 rs17253746 23

33 rs5962794 23

35 rs10782106 23
36 rs1506960 23
37 rs187781 23
38 rs217940 23
39 rs12390 23

45 rs5933183 23
46 rs7049614 23
47 rs2805901 23
48 rs205595 23
49 rs6653394 23
50 rs210584 23
51 rs4469624 23
52 rs768864 23
53 rs7051615 23
54 rs5983749 23

56 cnv13009p5 24

64 cnv13989PP1 24

66 cnv13993p1 24
67 cnv13993PP2 24
68 cnv13994PP3 24
69 rs7057320 24
70 rs7060372 24
71 cnv13998PP2 24
72 cnv13041p4 24
73 cnv13998PP5 24

75 cnv14005p9 24
76 rs28372113 24
77 cnv14010PP3 24
78 cnv13054PP1 24
79 cnv13054PP2 24
80 cnv14016PP2 24

82 cnv14019p72 24

85 rs6644784 24
86 cnv13077p12 24

89 cnv14048PP3 24
90 cnv14059PP2 24
91 rs2177730 24
92 rs2552960 24
93 rs2759868 24
94 rs2752082 25

96 cnvGap_CNV_14065.1p26 25
97 rs2444380 25
98 cnvGap_CNV_14068.2p41 25
99 rs2574308 25
100 rs2437539 25
101 rs35901717 25
102 rs34016119 25
103 rs9786041 25
104 cnvGap_CNV_14072.1p37 25
105 rs4475686 25
106 cnvGap_CNV_14075.3p45 25
107 cnv14091p87 25
108 rs9786877 25
109 rs4993477 25
110 rs9786745 25
111 rs13304191 25
112 rs7893091 25
113 cnv14130p17 25
114 cnv14136PP6 25
115 cnv14139p45 25
116 cnvcnv2007:405PP11 25
117 cnv14151p73 25
118 cnv14153p148 25
119 cnv14166p5 25
120 cnv14169p100 25
121 cnv14171PP2 25
122 cnv14183p236 25
123 cnv14197p58 25
124 cnvcnv2007:407PP180 25
125 cnvGap_CNV_14204.3PP137 25
126 cnvGap_CNV_14204.3PP230 25
127 rs2608382 25
128 cnvGap_CNV_14218.2p303 25
129 cnvGap_CNV_14218.2p1704 25
130 cnv14219p2 25
131 cnv14220p6 25
132 cnv14222p261 25
133 cnv14222p1441 25
134 rs2556648 25
135 cnv14222p2513 25
136 cnvGap_CNV_14222PP3088 25
137 cnv14222p3517 25
138 cnv14222p4202 25
139 cnv14222p4437 25
140 cnvGap_CNV_14222p2977 25
141 cnvGap_CNV_14222p3743 25
142 cnvGap_CNV_14222p3827 25
143 cnv14222PP15819 25
144 cnvGap_CNV_14222p6137 25
145 cnv14222PP18356 25
146 cnv14222PP20178 25
147 cnv14223p815 25
148 cnv14223p877 25
149 rs4134449 25
