## 43 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_28.csv 0.007939028 0.0071082391 0.3022200 0.003191489
## 44 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_68.csv 0.001931123 0.0006544503 0.3010537 0.264488936
## 45 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_75.csv 0.024782749 0.0242146597 0.2988150 0.272335845
##    reported_sex
## 17            M
## 18            M
## 42            M
## 43            M
## 44            F
## 45            F
pData(scanAnnot)[pData(scanAnnot)$family == 1344, ]
##    scanID subjectID family    father    mother CoriellID race sex status
## 19    298 200071490   1344 200116780 200005043   NA10851  CEU   M      0
## 20    299 200071490   1344 200116780 200005043   NA10851  CEU   M      0
## 38    317 200116780   1344         0         0   NA12056  CEU   M      1
## 39    318 200116780   1344         0         0   NA12056  CEU   M      1
## 40    319 200005043   1344         0         0   NA12057  CEU   F      1
## 41    320 200005043   1344         0         0   NA12057  CEU   F      1
##                                 genoRunID          plate batch
## 19 WG1000972-DNAB01-CIDR_10851@1007850566 WG0060472-AMP2     B
## 20 WG1000971-DNAA01-CIDR_10851@1007850374 WG0061537-AMP2     C
## 38 WG1000971-DNAB04-CIDR_12056@1007850430 WG0061538-AMP2     C
## 39 WG1000972-DNAC04-CIDR_12056@1007850435 WG0060473-AMP2     C
## 40 WG1000971-DNAC07-CIDR_12057@1007850454 WG0061539-AMP2     A
## 41 WG1000972-DNAD07-CIDR_12057@1007850405 WG0060474-AMP2     B
##                       file   missing.e1   missing.e2     het.A     het.X
## 19  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_8.csv 0.0022229279 0.0003231018 0.3028629 0.0000000
## 20  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_6.csv 0.0136551286 0.0109854604 0.3018584 0.0000000
## 38  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_7.csv 0.0012702445 0.0012924071 0.2597793 0.0000000
## 39  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_9.csv 0.0006351223 0.0006462036 0.2601504 0.0000000
## 40 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_62.csv 0.0009655616 0.0006544503 0.3159478 0.2736842
## 41 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_63.csv 0.0022529771 0.0009816754 0.3155779 0.2736842
##    reported_sex
## 19            M
## 20            M
## 38            M
## 39            M
## 40            F
## 41            F

Now that we’ve “corrected” the sample-swaps, we should make a new IBD plot to verify that observed and expected relationships now match.

#   Recalculate the relatedness as above

gds_geno <-
  system.file("extdata", "illumina_geno.gds", package = "GWASdata")
snps_to_include_for_kinship <-
  snpAnnot$snpID[snpAnnot$missing.n1 < 0.05]
gdsobj <- snpgdsOpen(gds_geno)
ibdobj <- snpgdsIBDKING(gdsobj, snp.id = snps_to_include_for_kinship)
## IBD analysis (KING method of moment) on genotypes:
## Excluding 1,339 SNPs (non-autosomes or non-selection)
## Excluding 35 SNPs (monomorphic: TRUE, MAF: NaN, missing rate: NaN)
##     # of samples: 77
##     # of SNPs: 1,926
##     using 1 thread
## No family is specified, and all individuals are treated as singletons.
## Relationship inference in the presence of population stratification.
## KING IBD:    the sum of all selected genotypes (0,1,2) = 132998
## CPU capabilities: Double-Precision SSE2
## Wed Feb  4 23:30:38 2026    (internal increment: 65536)
## [..................................................]  0%, ETC: ---        [==================================================] 100%, completed, 0s
## Wed Feb  4 23:30:38 2026    Done.
snpgdsClose(gdsobj)

#   Re-draw the pedigree

ped <-
  pData(scanAnnot)[, c("family", "subjectID", "father", "mother", "sex")]
dim(ped)
## [1] 77  5
names(ped) <- c("family", "individ", "father", "mother", "sex")
head(ped)
##   family   individ    father    mother sex
## 1   1341 200191449         0         0   F
## 2   1341 200191449         0         0   F
## 3   1341 200030290 200099417 200191449   F
## 4   1341 200030290 200099417 200191449   F
## 5   1341 200099417         0         0   M
## 6   1341 200099417         0         0   M
chk <- pedigreeCheck(ped)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
chk
## $duplicates
##    family   individ copies match
## 1    1341 200191449      2  TRUE
## 2    1341 200030290      2  TRUE
## 3    1341 200099417      2  TRUE
## 4    1341 200015835      2  TRUE
## 5    1340 200099636      2  TRUE
## 6    1340 200076256      2  TRUE
## 7    1408 200074814      2  TRUE
## 8    1408 200094287      2  TRUE
## 9    1408 200019401      2  TRUE
## 10   1334 200016815      2  TRUE
## 11   1334 200118596      2  TRUE
## 12   1334 200019634      2  TRUE
## 13   1344 200071490      2  TRUE
## 14   1344 200116780      2  TRUE
## 15   1344 200005043      2  TRUE
## 16   1347 200121301      2  TRUE
## 17   1347 200009887      2  TRUE
## 18   1347 200044196      2  TRUE
## 19   1362 200024383      2  TRUE
## 20   1362 200187448      2  TRUE
## 21   1362 200169440      2  TRUE
## 22   1362 200018192      2  TRUE
## 23   1362 200118709      2  TRUE
## 24      4 200047857      2  TRUE
## 25      4 200073630      2  TRUE
## 26      5 200102386      2  TRUE
## 27      5 200079101      2  TRUE
## 28      5 200022488      2  TRUE
## 29      9 200066330      2  TRUE
## 30      9 200033736      2  TRUE
## 31     12 200066777      2  TRUE
## 32     12 200160551      2  TRUE
## 33     28 200003216      2  TRUE
## 34     28 200034659      2  TRUE
## 
## $parent.no.individ.entry
##   row.num family no_individ_entry  parentID
## 1      13   1341           mother 200039107
## 2      14   1341           mother 200039107
## 
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
#   Remove duplicates, as before

dups <- chk$duplicates
head(dups)
##   family   individ copies match
## 1   1341 200191449      2  TRUE
## 2   1341 200030290      2  TRUE
## 3   1341 200099417      2  TRUE
## 4   1341 200015835      2  TRUE
## 5   1340 200099636      2  TRUE
## 6   1340 200076256      2  TRUE
uni.ped <- pedigreeDeleteDuplicates(ped, dups)
dim(uni.ped)
## [1] 43  5
chk <- pedigreeCheck(uni.ped)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
chk
## $parent.no.individ.entry
##   row.num family no_individ_entry  parentID
## 1       8   1341           mother 200039107
## 
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
## 
## $subfamilies.ident
##   family subfamily   individ
## 1   1362         1 200003297
## 2   1362         1 200169440
## 3   1362         1 200187448
## 6   1362         2 200018192
## 4   1362         2 200024383
## 5   1362         2 200118709
#   Recall there was a person whose mother needed to be added to the pedigree

ni <- chk$parent.no.individ.entry
ni
##   row.num family no_individ_entry  parentID
## 1       8   1341           mother 200039107
parent <-
  data.frame(
    family = ni$family,
    individ = ni$parentID,
    father = 0,
    mother = 0,
    sex = "F",
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
ped.updated <- rbind(uni.ped, parent)
chk <- pedigreeCheck(ped.updated)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
chk
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
## 
## $subfamilies.ident
##    family subfamily   individ
## 2    1341         1 200030290
## 3    1341         1 200099417
## 1    1341         1 200191449
## 5    1341         2 200015835
## 6    1341         2 200039107
## 4    1341         2 200122600
## 7    1362         1 200003297
## 8    1362         1 200169440
## 9    1362         1 200187448
## 12   1362         2 200018192
## 10   1362         2 200024383
## 11   1362         2 200118709
#   Recall that a family had to be broken into two new sub-families

ped.complete <- ped.updated
chk <- pedigreeCheck(ped.complete)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
subf <- chk$subfamilies.ident
subf
##    family subfamily   individ
## 2    1341         1 200030290
## 3    1341         1 200099417
## 1    1341         1 200191449
## 5    1341         2 200015835
## 6    1341         2 200039107
## 4    1341         2 200122600
## 7    1362         1 200003297
## 8    1362         1 200169440
## 9    1362         1 200187448
## 12   1362         2 200018192
## 10   1362         2 200024383
## 11   1362         2 200118709
table(subf$family)
## 
## 1341 1362 
##    6    6
subf.ids <- subf$individ[subf$subfamily == 2]
newfam <- ped.complete$individ %in% subf.ids
ped.complete$family[newfam] <-
  paste0(ped.complete$family[newfam], "-2")
table(ped.complete$family)
## 
##     12   1334   1340   1341 1341-2   1344   1347   1362 1362-2   1408     28 
##      3      3      3      3      3      3      3      3      3      3      3 
##      4      5     58   58-2      9 
##      3      3      1      1      3
pedigreeCheck(ped.complete)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
chk <- pedigreeCheck(ped.complete)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
chk
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
#   Earlier we split family 58 into two singleton families (the "father" and
#   "daughter" who weren't actually related) pedigreeCheck doesn't like
#   singletons, so we need to assign dummy parents for the original two members
#   of family 58 And these 4 dummy parents need to be added into the pedigree

onefams <- chk$one.person.fams
onefams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
ped.complete[ped.complete$family %in% c("58", "58-2"), ]
##    family   individ father mother sex
## 76     58 200122151      0      0   F
## 77   58-2 200105428      0      0   M
ped.complete[ped.complete$family == "58",  c("father", "mother")] <-
  c("1111", "2222")
ped.complete[ped.complete$family == "58-2", c("father", "mother")] <-
  c("3333", "4444")

parents <- data.frame(
  family = c("58", "58", "58-2", "58-2"),
  individ = c("1111", "2222", "3333", "4444"),
  father = c(0, 0, 0, 0),
  mother = c(0, 0, 0, 0),
  sex = c("M", "F", "M", "F"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

#   Complete the final pedigree, and now pedigreeCheck is happy (it returns NULL)

ped.final <- rbind(ped.complete, parents)
pedigreeCheck(ped.final)
## NULL
# Recalculate expected relationships in order to make a new plot, as above

ped.complete <- ped.final
rels <- pedigreePairwiseRelatedness(ped.final)
length(rels$inbred.fam)
## [1] 0
relprs <- rels$relativeprs
relprs[1:5, ]
##    Individ1  Individ2 relation kinship family
## 1 200191449 200030290       PO    0.25   1341
## 2 200191449 200099417        U    0.00   1341
## 3 200030290 200099417       PO    0.25   1341
## 4 200099636 200032162        U    0.00   1340
## 5 200099636 200076256       PO    0.25   1340
table(relprs$relation)
## 
## PO  U 
## 32 16
samp <- pData(scanAnnot)[, c("scanID", "subjectID")]
samp <- samp[match(ibdobj$sample.id, samp$scanID), ]
names(samp) <- c("scanID", "Individ")
ibd <- snpgdsIBDSelection(ibdobj)
ibd <- merge(ibd, samp, by.x = "ID1", by.y = "scanID")
ibd <-
  merge(
    ibd,
    samp,
    by.x = "ID2",
    by.y = "scanID",
    suffixes = c("1", "2")
  )
ibd$ii <- pasteSorted(ibd$Individ1, ibd$Individ2)
relprs$ii <- pasteSorted(relprs$Individ1, relprs$Individ2)
ibd <- merge(ibd, relprs[, c("ii", "relation")], all.x = TRUE)
names(ibd)[names(ibd) == "relation"] <- "exp.rel"
ibd$exp.rel[ibd$Individ1 == ibd$Individ2] <- "Dup"
ibd$exp.rel[is.na(ibd$exp.rel)] <- "U"
table(ibd$exp.rel, useNA = "ifany")
## 
##  Dup   PO    U 
##   34   90 2802
#   Use the ibdAssignRelatednessKing function to call the observed relationships
#   based on IBS0 and kinship values for each pair

ibd$obs.rel <- ibdAssignRelatednessKing(ibd$IBS0, ibd$kinship)
table(ibd$obs.rel, useNA = "ifany")
## 
## Deg3  Dup   PO    U 
##   64   34   90 2738
#   Observe that now the observed and expected relationships match (if we take
#   Deg3 and Unrelated as synonymous)

table(ibd$exp.rel, ibd$obs.rel)
##      
##       Deg3  Dup   PO    U
##   Dup    0   34    0    0
##   PO     0    0   90    0
##   U     64    0    0 2738
#   Finally, plot KC vs IBS0

#   Draw horizontal hresholds for assigning relationships using kinship
#   coefficients (taken from table 1 of Manichaikul (2010))

#   Now none of  the dots appear to be in the "wrong" clusters

cut.dup <- 1 / (2 ^ (3 / 2))
cut.deg1 <- 1 / (2 ^ (5 / 2))
cut.deg2 <- 1 / (2 ^ (7 / 2))
cut.deg3 <- 1 / (2 ^ (9 / 2))
cols <- c(Dup = "magenta",
          PO = "cyan",
          U = "black")
plot(ibd$IBS0,
     ibd$kinship,
     col = cols[ibd$exp.rel],
     xlab = "Fraction of IBS=0",
     ylab = "Kinship coefficient")
abline(
  h = c(cut.deg1, cut.deg2, cut.deg3, cut.dup),
  lty = 2,
  col = "gray"
)
legend("topright",
       legend = names(cols),
       col = cols,
       pch = 1)

Part 4 - Report (Relatedness)

  • Summarize the pedigree structures you observe in the data.

The pedigree data consists of parent-offspring trios with two samples per participant.

  • Describe what discrepancies you observe in the initial kinship vs IBS0 plot.

In the initial kinship vs IBS0 plot, it was observed that some unrelated samples were clustering as parent-offspring relationships while some parent-offspring relationships were clustering with unrelated samples. This gives the impression that at least some of this data may have been subjected to accidental swaps.

  • Explain exactly the kinds of swaps/discrepancies that were found, which IDs were involved, and exactly how you “fixed” them.

A. Participant 200122151 (F; scan 355) was reported as the daughter of participant 200105428 (M; scan 356) though they appear to be unrelated. To correct this, the male was moved to family “58-2” and given dummy mother 4444 and dummy father 3333. The female was left in family “58” and given dummy father 1111 and dummy mother 2222. Overall these were corrected by segregating them into their own pedigrees.

B. Participant 200016815 (scans 296 and 297) and 200071490 (scans 298 and 299) are suspected to be swapped as they both do NOT appear to be related to their parents, so we swapped their values for “family”, “father”, and “mother” to swap their pedigrees.

  • How would you recommend following up on these discrepancies “in real life” (instead of just assuming we know what happened and manually changing the data, which is a bad idea)?

Double check with the RA(s) to confirm the sample swap. Confirm with the PI if the case of unrelated father/daughter pair can be dropped as they do not have an extended family/mother for a trio.

  • Add a column to scanAnnot called “relationship.changes”, which briefly summarizes these “fixes” (add metadata for the column, too).
# view the discrepancy data that we generated above...
discrepany_fids # noted families of error
## [1] 1344 1334   58
discrepancy_list <- discrepancy_ids %>% # noted ids of error
  unique %>%
  unlist %>%
  sort
discrepancy_list
##  [1] 296 297 298 299 317 318 319 320 321 322 323 324 355 356
# overall discrepancies for...
# ...for the father/daughter no-relation...
no_relation <- discrepancies %>% 
  filter(ID2 == 356) %>% 
  select(ii) %>% 
  unique %>% 
  unlist
no_relation
##                    ii 
## "200105428/200122151"
# ...and dummy parents added
parents
##   family individ father mother sex
## 1     58    1111      0      0   M
## 2     58    2222      0      0   F
## 3   58-2    3333      0      0   M
## 4   58-2    4444      0      0   F
# and the finale pedigrees with dummies
ped.final %>% 
  filter(family == "58")
##    family   individ father mother sex
## 76     58 200122151   1111   2222   F
## 14     58      1111      0      0   M
## 2      58      2222      0      0   F
ped.final %>% 
  filter(family == "58-2")
##    family   individ father mother sex
## 77   58-2 200105428   3333   4444   M
## 31   58-2      3333      0      0   M
## 4    58-2      4444      0      0   F
# ...for the swaps
swapped_scans <- discrepancies %>% 
  filter(ID2 != 356) %>% 
  select(ii) %>% 
  unique %>% 
  unlist
swapped_scans
##                   ii1                   ii2                   ii3 
## "200005043/200016815" "200005043/200071490" "200016815/200019634" 
##                   ii4                   ii5                   ii6 
## "200016815/200116780" "200016815/200118596" "200019634/200071490" 
##                   ii7                   ii8 
## "200071490/200116780" "200071490/200118596"
# add new column "relationship.changes" to scanAnnot
annotate_relationship_changes <- function(row, swapped, unrelated) {
  ret_val <- "None"
  if (row["scanID"] %in% swapped) {
    ret_val <- "Unswapped"
  } else if(row["scanID"] %in% unrelated) {
    ret_val <- "Split Pedigree"
  }
  return(
    ret_val
  )
}

relationship_changes <- apply(
  scanAnnot %>% 
    getAnnotation(),
  1,
  annotate_relationship_changes,
  c(296, 297, 298, 299),
  c(355, 356)
)

scanAnnot$relationship.changes <- relationship_changes

# add annotation
varMetadata(scanAnnot)["relationship.changes", "labelDescription"] <-
  "logs manual changes made to scans whose pedigree data was updated"

# double check new column
scanAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(scanID %in% discrepancy_list)
##    scanID subjectID family    father    mother CoriellID race sex status
## 1     296 200016815   1334 200118596 200019634   NA10846  CEU   M      0
## 2     297 200016815   1334 200118596 200019634   NA10846  CEU   M      0
## 3     298 200071490   1344 200116780 200005043   NA10851  CEU   M      0
## 4     299 200071490   1344 200116780 200005043   NA10851  CEU   M      0
## 5     317 200116780   1344         0         0   NA12056  CEU   M      1
## 6     318 200116780   1344         0         0   NA12056  CEU   M      1
## 7     319 200005043   1344         0         0   NA12057  CEU   F      1
## 8     320 200005043   1344         0         0   NA12057  CEU   F      1
## 9     321 200118596   1334         0         0   NA12144  CEU   M      1
## 10    322 200118596   1334         0         0   NA12144  CEU   M      1
## 11    323 200019634   1334         0         0   NA12145  CEU   F      0
## 12    324 200019634   1334         0         0   NA12145  CEU   F      0
## 13    355 200122151     58         0         0   NA19222  YRI   F      0
## 14    356 200105428   58-2         0         0   NA19223  YRI   M      1
##                                 genoRunID          plate batch
## 1  WG1001172-DNAH05-CIDR_10846@1007850610 WG0063165-AMP2     C
## 2  WG1000991-DNAH04-CIDR_10846@1007850376 WG0058565-AMP2     B
## 3  WG1000972-DNAB01-CIDR_10851@1007850566 WG0060472-AMP2     B
## 4  WG1000971-DNAA01-CIDR_10851@1007850374 WG0061537-AMP2     C
## 5  WG1000971-DNAB04-CIDR_12056@1007850430 WG0061538-AMP2     C
## 6  WG1000972-DNAC04-CIDR_12056@1007850435 WG0060473-AMP2     C
## 7  WG1000971-DNAC07-CIDR_12057@1007850454 WG0061539-AMP2     A
## 8  WG1000972-DNAD07-CIDR_12057@1007850405 WG0060474-AMP2     B
## 9  WG1001172-DNAA08-CIDR_12144@1007850613 WG0063166-AMP2     C
## 10 WG1000991-DNAA07-CIDR_12144@1007850412 WG0058566-AMP2     A
## 11 WG1000991-DNAB10-CIDR_12145@1007850436 WG0058567-AMP2     B
## 12 WG1001172-DNAB11-CIDR_12145@1007850682 WG0063167-AMP2     C
## 13 WG1001000-DNAC07-CIDR_19222@1007850419 WG0061293-AMP2     A
## 14 WG1001000-DNAD10-CIDR_19223@1007850148 WG0061294-AMP2     A
##                       file   missing.e1   missing.e2     het.A       het.X
## 1  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_77.csv 0.0038107336 0.0006462036 0.2903226 0.000000000
## 2  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_27.csv 0.0073039060 0.0035541195 0.2905508 0.001057082
## 3   GENEVA_1M_HapMap_8.csv 0.0022229279 0.0003231018 0.3028629 0.000000000
## 4   GENEVA_1M_HapMap_6.csv 0.0136551286 0.0109854604 0.3018584 0.000000000
## 5   GENEVA_1M_HapMap_7.csv 0.0012702445 0.0012924071 0.2597793 0.000000000
## 6   GENEVA_1M_HapMap_9.csv 0.0006351223 0.0006462036 0.2601504 0.000000000
## 7  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_62.csv 0.0009655616 0.0006544503 0.3159478 0.273684211
## 8  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_63.csv 0.0022529771 0.0009816754 0.3155779 0.273684211
## 9  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_74.csv 0.0000000000 0.0000000000 0.3006012 0.000000000
## 10 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_28.csv 0.0079390283 0.0071082391 0.3022200 0.003191489
## 11 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_68.csv 0.0019311233 0.0006544503 0.3010537 0.264488936
## 12 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_75.csv 0.0247827486 0.0242146597 0.2988150 0.272335845
## 13 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_67.csv 0.0009655616 0.0006544503 0.2843531 0.296842105
## 14 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_65.csv 0.0022229279 0.0009693053 0.2918132 0.001052632
##    reported_sex relationship.changes
## 1             M            Unswapped
## 2             M            Unswapped
## 3             M            Unswapped
## 4             M            Unswapped
## 5             M                 None
## 6             M                 None
## 7             F                 None
## 8             F                 None
## 9             M                 None
## 10            M                 None
## 11            F                 None
## 12            F                 None
## 13            F       Split Pedigree
## 14            M       Split Pedigree

Part 5 - Principal component analysis (PCA) and population substructure

Here you’ll be introduced to performing PCA to uncover population substructure.

#   For input when we calculate PCA, we want to exclude SNPs from a few
#   problematic loci

#   Including SNPs from 2q21 (LCT), HLA, 8p23, and 17q21.31 can result PCs that
#   are highly correlated with these SNPs, which is undesirable

#   GWASTools supplies the positions of these loci in the relevant genome build
#   in a data frame called pcaSnpFilters.hg18

#   (Note that these loci are on chromosomes 2, 6, 8, and 17, which aren't
#   included in our genotyping data; we'll proceed in this example as if we had
#   genome-wide SNPs)

filt <- get(data(pcaSnpFilters.hg18))

#   Next we pull out the chromosomes, positions, and IDs of the SNPs in our
#   dataset

chrom <- getChromosome(snpAnnot)
pos <- getPosition(snpAnnot)
snpID <- getSnpID(snpAnnot)
snp.filt <- rep(TRUE, length(snpID))

#   We loop over the 4 regions to be excluded and identify which of our SNPs lie
#   with the endpoints of each

for (f in 1:nrow(filt)) {
  snp.filt[chrom == filt$chrom[f] &
             filt$start.base[f] < pos & pos < filt$end.base[f]] <- FALSE
}

#   Make a vector called snp.sel containing the snpIDs of SNPs not in the
#   problematic regions; these are the SNPs we'll retain for PCA

snp.sel <- snpID[snp.filt]
length(snp.sel)
## [1] 3300
#   (None of our 3300 SNPs get filtered out in this step, since they're not on
#   chromosomes 2, 6, 8, or 17)


### We also want to filter out some samples before running PCA

### Specifically, we shouldn't include duplicates (for subjects with multiple
### samples, we'll just pick one sample)

#   We'll re-order scanAnnot by missing.e1 so duplicate subjectIDs with a higher
#   missing rate are marked as duplicates

#   It's important that scanAnnot stays in the same order when we're done

#   Before changing the order, verify that scanAnnot is ordered by increasing
#   scanID (it is)

allequal(scanAnnot$scanID, sort(scanAnnot$scanID))
## [1] TRUE
#   Now re-order so that duplicate subjectIDs with a higher missing rate are
#   marked as duplicates

scanAnnot <-
  scanAnnot[order(scanAnnot$subjectID, scanAnnot$missing.e1), ]

#   Add a new column called "duplicated" indicating who the duplicates are

scanAnnot$duplicated <- duplicated(scanAnnot$subjectID)
table(scanAnnot$duplicated, useNA = "ifany")
## 
## FALSE  TRUE 
##    43    34
#   Put scanAnnot back in scanID order; this is very important!

scanAnnot <- scanAnnot[order(scanAnnot$scanID), ]
allequal(scanAnnot$scanID, sort(scanAnnot$scanID))
## [1] TRUE
#   Update the metadata

varMetadata(scanAnnot)["duplicated", "labelDescription"] <-
  "TRUE for duplicate scan with higher missing.e1"

#   Now make a vector of the scanIDs, leaving out the duplicates with higher
#   missingness (43 remain)

sample.sel <- scanAnnot$scanID[!scanAnnot$duplicated]
length(sample.sel)
## [1] 43
#   Note that in some applications you would only want to include unrelated
#   subjects when calculating PCs

#   In this exploratory example, we'll include parents and their children
#   instead of restricting ourselves to unrelated "founders""


### Now we can perform LD pruning, where independent SNPs are chosen using a
### "sliding window" along the chromosomes

### Re-open the genotypes (after making sure the previous connection is closed)

#close(genoData)
showfile.gds(closeall = T)
##                                                                                   FileName
## 1 /software/rhel9/manual/install/r/4.5.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_qxy.gds
## 2  /software/rhel9/manual/install/r/4.5.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_bl.gds
##   ReadOnly  State
## 1     TRUE closed
## 2     TRUE closed
gdsobj <- snpgdsOpen(genofile)

### Use the snpgdsLDpruning function

#   · sample.id/snp.id are used to retain only the samples/SNPs we picked out
#     above
#   · only autosomal SNPs are used
#   · a MAF threshold of 5% is applied
#   · the maximum window size we use is 1 megabase
#   · the LD threshold is set

#   Read the Details section of the help page for snpgdsLDpruning to understand
#   how the pruning algorithm works

snpset <- snpgdsLDpruning(
  gdsobj,
  sample.id = sample.sel,
  snp.id = snp.sel,
  autosome.only = TRUE,
  maf = 0.05,
  missing.rate = 0.05,
  method = "corr",
  slide.max.bp = 10e6,
  ld.threshold = sqrt(0.1)
)
## SNP pruning based on LD:
## Excluding 1,300 SNPs (non-autosomes or non-selection)
## Excluding 237 SNPs (monomorphic: TRUE, MAF: 0.05, missing rate: 0.05)
##     # of samples: 43
##     # of SNPs: 1,763
##     using 1 thread
##     sliding window: 10,000,000 basepairs, Inf SNPs
##     |LD| threshold: 0.316228
##     method: correlation
## Chrom 21: |====================|====================|
##     8.30%, 83 / 1,000 (Wed Feb  4 23:30:39 2026)
## Chrom 22: |====================|====================|
##     8.90%, 89 / 1,000 (Wed Feb  4 23:30:39 2026)
## 172 markers are selected in total.
snp.pruned <- unlist(snpset, use.names = FALSE)
length(snp.pruned)
## [1] 172
#   The output is a list the SNPs on each chromosome

#   (Roughly 180 SNPs are retained. Note that this procedure includes a random
#   process, so the number of SNPs selected will vary)


#   Now we calculate the PCs by applying snpgdsPCA, using only the pruned SNPs
#   and the samples selected earlier

pca <- snpgdsPCA(gdsobj, sample.id = sample.sel, snp.id = snp.pruned)
## Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on genotypes:
## Excluding 3,128 SNPs (non-autosomes or non-selection)
## Excluding 0 SNP (monomorphic: TRUE, MAF: NaN, missing rate: NaN)
##     # of samples: 43
##     # of SNPs: 172
##     using 1 thread
##     # of principal components: 32
## PCA:    the sum of all selected genotypes (0,1,2) = 6055
## CPU capabilities: Double-Precision SSE2
## Wed Feb  4 23:30:39 2026    (internal increment: 55532)
## [..................................................]  0%, ETC: ---        [==================================================] 100%, completed, 0s
## Wed Feb  4 23:30:39 2026    Begin (eigenvalues and eigenvectors)
## Wed Feb  4 23:30:39 2026    Done.
dim(pca$eigenvect)
## [1] 43 32
head(pca$eigenvect)
##             [,1]        [,2]         [,3]        [,4]        [,5]        [,6]
## [1,] -0.12425805 -0.16475508  0.032424555 -0.09422899 -0.05341488 -0.10193462
## [2,] -0.09900284 -0.29516937  0.033184298 -0.02512715  0.16045701 -0.03244578
## [3,] -0.10010405 -0.34065011 -0.006955399 -0.02958605  0.21361130  0.03569541
## [4,] -0.08904642 -0.08785714 -0.043350402  0.02116387 -0.06464645 -0.11476937
## [5,] -0.26881676 -0.23872606 -0.002685151  0.08267022  0.25839847 -0.06429048
## [6,] -0.22550964 -0.20228361  0.025490552  0.06950088  0.12495786 -0.19495688
##             [,7]        [,8]       [,9]       [,10]       [,11]       [,12]
## [1,] -0.10249479  0.06615565  0.2769473 -0.08913642  0.03844867  0.17050088
## [2,]  0.01866334  0.06261581  0.2694993 -0.15521457  0.03641987 -0.04587859
## [3,]  0.03954789  0.11059492  0.1100174 -0.07326776  0.06554750 -0.17377927
## [4,] -0.07911219 -0.03912288 -0.1862203 -0.09729197 -0.14733622  0.34780713
## [5,]  0.07410880  0.08666436 -0.1935811  0.07210379 -0.02762008  0.03613251
## [6,]  0.04036008  0.05630801 -0.2076434 -0.05741736 -0.09596775  0.15379410
##           [,13]       [,14]       [,15]       [,16]       [,17]       [,18]
## [1,]  0.2483164  0.01467593  0.31067202  0.12093078 -0.00289679  0.08263519
## [2,]  0.2959004  0.12140826  0.24656567 -0.01520432  0.03644040 -0.13771344
## [3,]  0.2020166  0.06245052  0.01045491 -0.10674047  0.13738272 -0.15824484
## [4,] -0.1279016 -0.07755329  0.06264407  0.34142145 -0.04306024 -0.01436521
## [5,] -0.1517800  0.06787021 -0.12636097 -0.18198462  0.23370546  0.04884608
## [6,] -0.2769728 -0.04079561  0.01924604  0.17900324  0.14909731  0.07650599
##            [,19]       [,20]       [,21]        [,22]       [,23]       [,24]
## [1,] -0.10613627 -0.03007089 -0.30044801  0.063705778 -0.21764658  0.37200869
## [2,]  0.06823188  0.08629799 -0.08971288 -0.077903629 -0.01883737  0.00503591
## [3,]  0.14397958  0.04494914  0.16006771  0.007802113  0.16967281 -0.40830653
## [4,]  0.03962743  0.46848974 -0.21861046 -0.031983690  0.14776606 -0.24620039
## [5,] -0.26759150 -0.18397306  0.22018958  0.169717531 -0.11855460  0.23991001
## [6,] -0.20747546  0.13861068  0.06314762  0.002452803  0.04387914  0.11174166
##             [,25]       [,26]       [,27]       [,28]       [,29]       [,30]
## [1,]  0.036091667  0.08837448 -0.07222202  0.23316639  0.02120004  0.15827422
## [2,]  0.006600723  0.09725029  0.07283754 -0.14267073  0.08432036 -0.08776895
## [3,] -0.096418939  0.02016162  0.13507951 -0.15830244 -0.16608686 -0.14907249
## [4,]  0.130664983 -0.09981173  0.10254053 -0.01101293  0.01187916 -0.05708975
## [5,] -0.117883717  0.01001958 -0.09173351  0.02355549  0.01674408  0.04151422
## [6,] -0.042178779 -0.03960253 -0.04745646 -0.09966648 -0.16564064 -0.06731836
##            [,31]       [,32]
## [1,]  0.09306213  0.32822050
## [2,] -0.24208811 -0.28595625
## [3,]  0.23964693  0.25287709
## [4,]  0.19396854 -0.05513924
## [5,]  0.24856597 -0.12217889
## [6,] -0.39156878 -0.04391753
#   The function returns a list of 8 objects, including the eigenvectors and the
#   ordered scanIDs

#   32 eigenvectors/PCs have been provided (in order); they're stored in
#   pca$eigenvect, a 43x32 matrix (43 samples by 32 eigenvectors)

Part 5 - Report (PCAs)

  • How many SNPs were used to calculate PCs?

176 SNPs

  • Make pairwise scatterplots of the first 4 PCs, using different plotting symbols for the two populations, as reported in the “race” variable in scanAnnot.

View below code chunk output

  • Make a parallel coordinate plot for the first 4 plots as well, again distinguishing the plotted lines according to the “race” variable.

View below code chunkoutput

  • What information does the first PC appear to convey?

The first PC appears to convey that we will want to account for ancestry in our GWAS as the different populations are clustering among themselves.

library(GGally)
## Registered S3 method overwritten by 'GGally':
##   method from   
##   +.gg   ggplot2
library(patchwork)
library(cowplot)
## 
## Attaching package: 'cowplot'
## The following object is masked from 'package:patchwork':
## 
##     align_plots
## The following object is masked from 'package:lubridate':
## 
##     stamp
# create a copy of pca and add race to it
pca_with_race <- pca
scans <- scanAnnot %>% getAnnotation()
pca_with_race$race <- scans[scans$scanID %in% pca$sample.id,]$race %>% unlist

# create a function to generate the data frames and the PCAs to be used
generate_pca_dataframe <- function(data, pcax, pcay) {
  df <- as.data.frame(
    list(
      "scanID" = unlist(data$sample.id),
      "PCAX" = data[["eigenvect"]][,pcax],
      "PCAY" = data[["eigenvect"]][,pcay],
      "race" = unlist(data$race)
    )
  )
  return(
    list(
      "data" = df,
      "x" = paste0("PCA ", toString(pcax)),
      "y" = paste0("PCA ", toString(pcay))
    )
  )
}

# create each set of PCAs to be combined for plotting
generate_pca_indices <- function(num) {
  scatterplot_rows <- list()
  for(i in 1:4) {
    scatterplot_rows[[i]] <- setdiff(c(1:4), c(i)) %>% sort
  }
  return(
    scatterplot_rows
  )
}

# function to generate all PCA dataframes
generate_all_pca_dataframes <- function(data, indices) {
  dfs <- list()
  for(row in 1:length(indices)) {
    data_row <- list()
    for(cell in 1:length(indices[[row]])) {
      data_row[[cell]] <- generate_pca_dataframe(data, row, indices[[row]][[cell]])
    }
    dfs[[row]] <- data_row
  }
  return(
    dfs
  )
}

# function to plot a single PCA given a plotting function
plot_pca <- function(pca_data, with_legend = FALSE) {
  plot <- pca_data[["data"]] %>% 
    ggplot() +
    aes(
      x = PCAX,
      y = PCAY,
      color = race
    ) + 
    geom_point() +
    xlab(pca_data[["x"]]) +
    ylab(pca_data[["y"]]) +
    theme_minimal()
  if(!with_legend) {
    plot <- plot + theme(legend.position = "none")
  }
  return(
    plot
  )
}

# function to get the legend
# how to save the legend:
# https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12041042/how-to-plot-just-the-legends-in-ggplot2
get_pca_legend <- function(pca_df) {
  pca_plot <- plot_pca(pca_df, TRUE)
  plot_legend <- cowplot::get_legend(pca_plot)
  return(
    plot_legend
  )
}

# function to plot all PCAs with a given plotting function
plot_all_PCAs <- function(data) {
  row_plots <- list()
  plot_legend <- get_pca_legend(data[[1]][[1]])
  for(pca_row in 1:length(data)) {
    row_plot <- NA
    for(pca_cell in 1:length(data[[pca_row]])) {
      if (pca_cell == 1) {
        row_plot <- plot_pca(
          data[[pca_row]][[pca_cell]]
        )
      } else {
        plot <- plot_pca(
          data[[pca_row]][[pca_cell]]
        )
        row_plot = row_plot | plot
      }
    }
    row_plots[[pca_row]] <- row_plot
  }
  final_plot <- row_plots[[1]]
  for(row in 2:length(row_plots)) {
    final_plot <- final_plot / row_plots[[row]]
  }
  return(
    final_plot / plot_legend
  )
}

# 0. generate the dataframes for each PCA pair of the first 4 PCAs
# a. generate the rows indices for 4 PCAS
pca_indices <- generate_pca_indices(4)
# b. generate the actual dataframes
pca_dfs <- generate_all_pca_dataframes(pca_with_race, pca_indices)

# 1. pairwise scatterplots of the first 4 PCs
#   - use different plotting symbols for the two populations "race"
#   - use geom_point from ggplot
pca_scatter <- plot_all_PCAs(pca_dfs)
pca_scatter

# 2. parallel coordinate plot for the first 4 PCs
#   - use ggparcoord from GGally

# generate a dataframe to use with this function
pca_df <- pca$eigenvect[,1:4] %>% 
  as.tibble() %>% 
  rename(
    PCA1 = V1,
    PCA2 = V2,
    PCA3 = V3,
    PCA4 = V4
  )
## Warning: `as.tibble()` was deprecated in tibble 2.0.0.
## ℹ Please use `as_tibble()` instead.
## ℹ The signature and semantics have changed, see `?as_tibble`.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.
## Warning: The `x` argument of `as_tibble.matrix()` must have unique column names if
## `.name_repair` is omitted as of tibble 2.0.0.
## ℹ Using compatibility `.name_repair`.
## ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the tibble package.
##   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/tidyverse/tibble/issues>.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.
pca_df$sampleID <- pca$sample.id
pca_df$race <- pca_with_race$race

# generate the table
# https://ggobi.github.io/ggally/reference/ggparcoord.html
ggparcoord(
  data = pca_df,
  columns = 1:4,
  groupColumn = "race"
)

Part 6 - Duplicate Sample Discordance

Here we leverage the duplicate samples to assess genotyping errors. If the same probe often yields different genotypes for duplicate scans from the same subjects, we should probably exclude the genotypes called from that probe. We will use the function duplicateDiscordance.

#   First, we should only use high-quality scans to when we judge the probes

scan.excl <- scanAnnot$scanID[scanAnnot$missing.e1 >= 0.05]
length(scan.excl)
## [1] 0
#   (Recall that none of our scans had high missingness, so we don't have to
#   exclude anyone in this dataset)

#   We also don't want to bother testing for discordance in SNPs that are 100%
#   missing, so we exclude those, too

snp.excl <- snpAnnot$snpID[snpAnnot$missing.n1 == 1]
length(snp.excl)
## [1] 151
#   Get the genotype data (make sure the connection to the file is closed before
#   reopening it)

#close(genoData)
showfile.gds(closeall = T, verbose = T)
##                                                                                    FileName
## 1 /software/rhel9/manual/install/r/4.5.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_geno.gds
##   ReadOnly  State
## 1     TRUE closed
genofile <-
  system.file("extdata", "illumina_geno.gds", package = "GWASdata")
genoGDS <- GdsGenotypeReader(genofile)

#   Attach the annotation to it (note that the sex/relatedness information were
#   "fixed" above)

genoData <-
  GenotypeData(genoGDS, snpAnnot = snpAnnot, scanAnnot = scanAnnot)

#   Now we use the dupdisc function to calculate the discordance for each SNP
#   (while excluding any low-quality scans and 100%-missing SNPs)

dupdisc <-
  duplicateDiscordance(
    genoData,
    subjName.col = "subjectID",
    scan.exclude = scan.excl,
    snp.exclude = snp.excl
  )
## subject 1 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 2 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 3 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 4 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 5 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 6 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 7 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 8 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 9 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 10 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 11 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 12 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 13 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 14 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 15 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 16 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 17 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 18 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 19 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 20 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 21 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 22 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 23 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 24 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 25 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 26 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 27 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 28 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 29 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 30 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 31 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 32 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 33 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 34 out of 34, 2 replications
#   The function returns a list of 3 objects: discordance.by.snp,
#   discordance.by.subject, and correlation.by.subject

names(dupdisc)
## [1] "discordance.by.snp"     "discordance.by.subject" "correlation.by.subject"
dim(dupdisc$discordance.by.snp)
## [1] 3149    5
head(dupdisc$discordance.by.snp)
##    snpID discordant npair n.disc.subj discord.rate
## 1 999465          0    33           0            0
## 2 999493          0    31           0            0
## 3 999512          0    34           0            0
## 4 999561          0    34           0            0
## 5 999567          0    34           0            0
## 6 999569          0    34           0            0
#   discordance.by.snp has a row for each SNP, detailing the number of
#   duplicate-pairs of scans observed for that SNP, the number of discordant
#   gentoypes observed among those pairs, and the discordance rate

length(dupdisc$discordance.by.subject)
## [1] 34
#   discordance.by.subject is a list of matrices. For each subject with
#   duplicate scans, the matrix shows the discordance between the pairs of scans
#   (i.e., at what proportion of SNPs do two scans from the same person have
#   different genotypes?)

dupdisc$discordance.by.subject[[1]]
##     280 281
## 280   0   0
## 281   0   0
#   For the first subject in dupdisc$discordance.by.subject (scans 280/281), it
#   looks like the genotypes agreed for all the SNPs

dupdisc$discordance.by.subject[[2]]
##              282          283
## 282 0.0000000000 0.0003265839
## 283 0.0003265839 0.0000000000
#   For the second subject (scans 282/283), the discordance rate was
#   0.0003265839


#   Save a summary of this information by merging the discordances into the SNP
#   annotation

snpAnnot$dummy <- 1:nrow(pData(snpAnnot))
pData(snpAnnot) <-
  merge(
    x = pData(snpAnnot),
    y = dupdisc$discordance.by.snp[, c("snpID", "discordant")],
    all.x = T,
    all.y = F
  )

#   Make sure the order of the SNPs is maintained

pData(snpAnnot) <- pData(snpAnnot)[order(snpAnnot$dummy), ]

Part 6 - Report

Briefly explain the purpose of this step and what it means to check for discordant genotypes. Summarize the results. Be sure to answer the following:

The purpose of this step is to ensure that we are getting consistent genotyping across intended duplicates. In the case of our genotyping results not being consistent, then we typically filter out these SNPs.

  • How many SNPs are discordant in at least one duplicate-pair?

28

  • What’s the maximum number of discordances seen for any SNP?

2 -> for SNPs: 1026261 1028039 1070217

  • What’s the median discordance rate? What’s the maximum discordance rate?

median: 0 max: 0.07407407

  • Decide and explicitly state the threshold, n, for filtering out SNPs based on discordance (i.e., SNPs with dupdisc$discordance.by.snp >= n will be removed)

30 ≤ discordant pairs < 75, so n ≥ 1 will be our SNP filtering threshold based on discordance.

  • You may find it helpful to call the function duplicateDiscordanceProbability(npair=34). Read the documentation for this function.

  • Of the SNPs pass that passed the missing.n2 filter (missing.n2 < 0.05), how many SNPs pass/fail the discordance threshold?

fail: 21 pass: 3074

  • Of the SNPs with missing.n2 < 0.05, make a histogram of the discordance rate

View the output of the below code chunk.

  • Make sure you add a new column to snpAnnot called failed.dup.disc (“pass” if the SNP had >=n discordant calls, “fail” if it failed, “untested” if it was one of the high-missingness SNPs that we excluded); update the metadata
#-   How many SNPs are discordant in at least one duplicate-pair?
# these appear to be 1 is discordant...just sum them
sum(dupdisc$discordance.by.snp$discordant)
## [1] 28
#-   What's the maximum number of discordances seen for any SNP?
max_disc_for_any_snp <- max(dupdisc$discordance.by.snp$n.disc.subj)
max_disc_for_any_snp
## [1] 2
dupdisc$discordance.by.snp %>% 
  filter(n.disc.subj == max_disc_for_any_snp)
##     snpID discordant npair n.disc.subj discord.rate
## 1 1026261          2    27           2   0.07407407
## 2 1028039          2    30           2   0.06666667
## 3 1070217          2    27           2   0.07407407
#-   What's the median discordance rate? What's the maximum discordance rate?
dupdisc$discordance.by.snp %>%
  summarise(
    med = median(discord.rate),
    max = max(discord.rate)
  )
##   med        max
## 1   0 0.07407407
#-   You may find it helpful to call the function duplicateDiscordanceProbability(npair=34)
duplicateDiscordanceProbability(npair=34)
##        error=1e-05  error=1e-04  error=0.001   error=0.01
## dis>0 6.797706e-04 6.777101e-03 6.575540e-02 4.942376e-01
## dis>1 2.243009e-07 2.234113e-05 2.147291e-03 1.459858e-01
## dis>2 4.784862e-11 4.763922e-08 4.559737e-05 2.961468e-02
## dis>3 7.366668e-15 7.382418e-11 7.051524e-07 4.476031e-03
## dis>4 0.000000e+00 8.858958e-14 8.452530e-09 5.304432e-04
## dis>5 0.000000e+00 9.581360e-17 8.167288e-11 5.100698e-05
## dis>6 0.000000e+00 1.025893e-17 6.533692e-13 4.077516e-06
## dis>7 0.000000e+00 1.019048e-17 4.402201e-15 2.758531e-07
#-   Make sure you add a new column to snpAnnot called failed.dup.disc ("pass" if the SNP had \>=n discordant calls, "fail" if it failed, "untested" if it was one of the high-missingness SNPs that we excluded); update the metadata
failed_dup_disc <- snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  mutate(
    failed.dup.disc = 
      if_else(
        is.na(discordant),
        "untested",
        if_else(
          discordant > 0,
          "fail",
          "pass"
        )
      )
  ) %>% 
  select(failed.dup.disc) %>% 
  unlist()
snpAnnot$failed.dup.disc <- failed_dup_disc

varMetadata(snpAnnot)["failed.dup.disc", "labelDescription"] <-
  "\"pass\" if the SNP had >=n discordant calls, \"fail\" if it failed, \"untested\" if it was one of the high-missingness SNPs that we excluded"

#-   Of the SNPs pass that passed the missing.n2 filter (missing.n2 \<0.05), how many SNPs pass/fail the discordance threshold?
pass_missing <- snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.n2 < 0.05)
pass_missing %>% 
  group_by(failed.dup.disc) %>% 
  summarise(
    n = n()
  )
## # A tibble: 2 × 2
##   failed.dup.disc     n
##   <chr>           <int>
## 1 fail               21
## 2 pass             3074
#-   Of the SNPs with missing.n2 \< 0.05, make a histogram of the discordance rate
pass_missing_w_disc <- pass_missing %>% 
  left_join(
    dupdisc$discordance.by.snp,
    by = "snpID"
  )
pass_missing_w_disc %>% 
  ggplot() +
  aes(
    x = discord.rate
  ) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01)
## Warning: <ggplot> %+% x was deprecated in ggplot2 4.0.0.
## ℹ Please use <ggplot> + x instead.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

Part 7 - Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium (HWE) testing

Deviations from HWE can indicate poor genotyping. Here you will appropriately test for departures from HWE in the data using exactHWE (which applies Fisher’s exact test).

#   The test we'll use for HWE will assume independent observations

#   Therefore we should include only one scan from each subject

#   We should also include only "founders" (for this example, that means
#   excluding the children, whose genotypes aren't independent of their
#   parents')

#   The nonfounders are the individuals who have a mother or father in the
#   pedigree

head(pData(scanAnnot)[, c("father", "mother")])
##      father    mother
## 1         0         0
## 2         0         0
## 3 200099417 200191449
## 4 200099417 200191449
## 5         0         0
## 6         0         0
nonfounders <- scanAnnot$father != 0 & scanAnnot$mother != 0
table(nonfounders)
## nonfounders
## FALSE  TRUE 
##    51    26
#   HWE is sensitive to population stratification (A SNP can be in HWE in each
#   of two populations, but out of HWE in the combined population)

#   Therefore in this example we'll focus on HWE in just one population group,
#   the "CEU" group

#   So we'll exclude populations other than CEU, nonfounders, and duplicates
#   (recall tha that the "duplicated" variable was defined to include the scan
#   with better missingness for each subject)

scan.excl <-
  scanAnnot$scanID[scanAnnot$race != "CEU" |
                     nonfounders | scanAnnot$duplicated]
length(scan.excl)
## [1] 60
#   Now we use exactHWE to calculate HWE statistics

#   We do this separately for the sex chromosomes

chr <- getChromosome(genoData)
auto <- range(which(chr %in% 1:22))
X <- range(which(chr == 23))

#   exactHWE takes snpStart/snpEnd arguments; we use these to identify the
#   indices corresponding to autosomal/sex chromosome SNPs

hwe <-
  exactHWE(
    genoData,
    scan.exclude = scan.excl,
    snpStart = auto[1],
    snpEnd = auto[2]
  )
## Beginning Calculations...
## Block 1 of 1 Completed - 0.01031 secs
hweX <-
  exactHWE(
    genoData,
    scan.exclude = scan.excl,
    snpStart = X[1],
    snpEnd = X[2]
  )
## Beginning Calculations...
## Block 1 of 1 Completed - 0.001305 secs
#   We use the scan.exclude argument to exclude non-CEU, non-founders, and duplicates

#   exactHWE has returned a data frame of HWE statistics for each SNP; read the help page to see what the columns are

#   Attach the two data frames

hwe <- rbind(hwe, hweX)
names(hwe)
## [1] "snpID"        "chr"          "nAA"          "nAB"          "nBB"         
## [6] "MAF"          "minor.allele" "f"            "pval"
#close(genoData)

#   Check on sample sizes for autosomes

hwe$N <- hwe$nAA + hwe$nAB + hwe$nBB
summary(hwe$N[is.element(hwe$chr, 1:22)])
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##    0.00   17.00   17.00   16.93   17.00   17.00
#   ... and for the X chromosome

summary(hwe$N[is.element(hwe$chr, 23)])
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   0.000   8.000   8.000   7.597   8.000   8.000
#   Notice there are some missing p-values for the autosomes

#   ... and for X

#   We can view some of the genotype counts

#   The first SNP appears to be 100% missing, and the second is monomorphic
#   (i.e., MAF=0)

#   (recall that we didn't filter out SNPs here)

hwe$pval[1:10]
##  [1]        NA        NA 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 0.5372636 0.1788856
##  [8] 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000
sum(is.na(hwe$pval[hwe$chr == 23]))
## [1] 300
head(hwe[, c("nAA", "nAB", "nBB")])
##   nAA nAB nBB
## 1   0   0   0
## 2   0   0  17
## 3   0   4  11
## 4   1   7   9
## 5   0   2  15
## 6  11   5   1
#   Now we want to inspect deviation from HWE

#   We can use Q-Q plots to help with this

#   We should exclude monomorphic SNPs

hwe.0 <- hwe[hwe$MAF > 0, ]
dim(hwe.0)
## [1] 2539   10
#   Only keep the autosomal SNPs for first plot

pval <- hwe.0$pval[is.element(hwe.0$chr, 1:22)]
length(pval)
## [1] 1785
#   Double-check that there are no missing p-values remaining

pval <- pval[!is.na(pval)]
length(pval)
## [1] 1785
#   Similarly, get the p-values for the X-chromosome

pval.x <- hwe.0$pval[is.element(hwe.0$chr, 23)]
length(pval.x)
## [1] 700
pval.x <- pval.x[!is.na(pval.x)]
length(pval.x)
## [1] 700
#   Now make Q-Q plots

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
qqPlot(pval = pval,
       truncate = FALSE,
       main = "Autosomes, all")
qqPlot(pval = pval,
       truncate = TRUE,
       main = "Autosomes, truncated")
qqPlot(pval = pval.x,
       truncate = FALSE,
       main = "X chromosome, all")
qqPlot(pval = pval.x,
       truncate = TRUE,
       main = "X chromosome, truncated")

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Part 7 - Report

Briefly explain the purpose of HWE testing. In describing your results for this step, answer the folowing:

Overall, we generally presume that genotypes will follow HWE, so here we are looking to drop any that do not.

  • How do you choose what subjects to include for HWE testing? How many were used here?

Here, we focused solely on the CEU population as HWE testing is sensitive to population stratification. We also only included founders as we must presume that all samples are independent and in having parent-offspring triads we know that this is not the case.

  • Interpret the Q-Q plots. Do any SNPs appear to be strongly out of HWE? Which SNPs? What are the lowest p-values?

SNPs appear to be strongly out of HWE for the X chromosome and autosomes. These are the SNPs that do not fall on the line (do not have an observed p-value near their expected p-value). The lowest p-values are the SNPs higher on the y-axis. Overall, the X chromosome appears much worse off.

  • For the autosomal SNPs, make a plot of -log10(p-value) vs MAF. Make another such plot for the X chromosome.

See output of the below code chunk.

  • What p-value will you use for filtering our SNPs with low HWE? Of the SNPs with missing.n2 < 0.05, how many will be retained/removed?

See output of the below code chunk. retained 2454
removed 1
NA 640

  • Add the HWE p-values to the SNP annotation (and update the metadata)

See output of the below code chunk.

# -   For the autosomal SNPs, make a plot of -log10(p-value) vs MAF.
hwe.auto <- hwe %>% 
  filter(chr %in% 1:22) %>% 
  drop_na()
hwe.auto %>% 
  ggplot() +
  aes(
    x = MAF,
    y = -log10(pval)
  ) +
  geom_point()

# -   Make another such plot for the X chromosome.
hwe.x <- hwe %>% 
  filter(chr == 23) %>% 
  drop_na()
hwe.x %>% 
  ggplot() +
  aes(
    x = MAF,
    y = -log10(pval)
  ) +
  geom_point()

# -   Add the HWE p-values to the SNP annotation (and update the metadata)
pval_threshold <- 10^(-4)
with_pval <- snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  full_join(
    hwe,
    by = "snpID"
  ) %>%
  select(pval) %>% 
  unlist
snpAnnot$pval <- with_pval

varMetadata(snpAnnot)["pval", "labelDescription"] <-
  "NA if p-value not calculate, otherwise the calculated HWE p-value."

# -   What p-value will you use for filtering our SNPs with low HWE?
# Of the SNPs with missing.n2 \< 0.05, how many will be retained/removed?
pval_threshold
## [1] 1e-04
final <- snpAnnot %>% 
  getAnnotation() %>% 
  filter(missing.n2 < 0.05)
final %>%
  mutate(
    pval_filter = if_else(pval > pval_threshold, "retained", "removed")
  ) %>% 
  group_by(pval_filter) %>% 
  summarise(
    n = n()
  )
## # A tibble: 3 × 2
##   pval_filter     n
##   <chr>       <int>
## 1 removed         1
## 2 retained     2454
## 3 <NA>          640
